title: EXPOSÉ VII A INFINITESIMAL STUDY OF GROUP SCHEMES

pages.title: EXPOSÉ VII A INFINITESIMAL STUDY OF GROUP SCHEMES

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ VII A
INFINITESIMAL STUDY OF GROUP SCHEMESby P. Gabriel

In Exposé II we restricted ourselves to the study of first-order differential invariants and did not touch upon certain phenomena special to characteristic \(p > 0\) or to characteristic 0. Our aim in part A of this exposé is to fill this gap. Moreover, the infinitesimal study of arbitrary order of a group scheme is related to that of the associated formal group; the aim of the second part of this exposé is to present the first definitions and properties concerning formal groups.

pages.title: A) Differential operators and p-Lie algebras(*)

blocks.html: A) Differential operators and p-Lie algebras(*)
(*) Part A of the present exposé had not been treated seriously in the oral exposés.
1. Differential operators

In the following paragraphs 1, 2 and 3, S denotes a scheme and the products considered are cartesian products in the category of S-schemes. If X is an S-scheme, we denote by \(p_{X/S}\), \(p_X\) or simply \(p\) the structural morphism from X to S.

Let \(u : Y \to X\) be a morphism of S-schemes and let us equip the direct image \(u_*(\mathscr{O}_Y)\) of the structure sheaf of Y with the \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module structure induced by \(u\). The sheaf \(\mathscr{H} = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{p_X^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_S)}(\mathscr{O}_X, u_*(\mathscr{O}_Y))\) of homomorphisms of \(p_X^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_S)\)-Modules from \(\mathscr{O}_X\) to \(u_*(\mathscr{O}_X)\) is thus naturally equipped with an \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-bi-Module structure: if U is an open subset of X, \(f\) and \(d\) sections of \(\mathscr{O}_X\) and \(\mathscr{H}\) over U, then \(fd\) and \(df\) are respectively the morphisms \(x \mapsto f\,d(x)\) and \(x \mapsto d(fx)\) from \(\mathscr{O}_X\) to \(u_*(\mathscr{O}_Y)\). We shall henceforth write \((\mathrm{ad}\,f)d\) instead of \(fd - df\).

An S-deviation of order \(\leqslant n\) is by definition a pair \(\mathrm{D} = (u,d)\) consisting of a morphism of S-schemes \(u : Y \to X\) and a morphism of \(p^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_S)\)-Modules \(d : \mathscr{O}_X \to u_*(\mathscr{O}_Y)\) such that \[ (\mathrm{ad}\,f_0)(\mathrm{ad}\,f_1)\cdots(\mathrm{ad}\,f_n)d = 0 \tag{\(*\)} \] for every open subset U of X and all sequences of \(n+1\) sections \(f_0,\ldots,f_n\) of \(\mathscr{O}_X\) over U. If the equalities \((*)\) are satisfied, we shall also say that \(d\) is an S-deviation of \(u\) of order \(\leqslant n\). In particular, an S-deviation of order \(\leqslant 0\) is a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules from \(\mathscr{O}_X\) to \(u_*(\mathscr{O}_Y)\).

A morphism of \(p^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_S)\)-Modules \(d\) from \(\mathscr{O}_X\) to \(u_*(\mathscr{O}_Y)\) is an S-deviation of \(u\) if, for every point \(y\) of Y, there exist an open neighborhood U of \(u(y)\) in X and an open neighborhood V of \(y\) in Y satisfying the following conditions: a) \(u(\mathrm{V}) \subset \mathrm{U}\); b) if \(v : \mathrm{V} \to \mathrm{U}\) is the morphism induced by \(u\), there is an integer \(n\) such that the morphism \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{U} \to v_*(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{V})\) induced by \(d\) is an S-deviation of order \(\leqslant n\). If \(d\) is an S-deviation of \(u\), we also say that the pair \(\mathrm{D} = (u,d)\) is an S-deviation and we shall sometimes write \(\mathrm{Y} \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{D}\ } \mathrm{X}\) or \(\mathrm{Y} \xrightarrow[u]{\ d\ } \mathrm{X}\). When \(d\) is the algebra homomorphism that defines \(u\), we shall also write \(u\) instead of D.
Let us now consider two S-deviations \(\mathrm{D} = (u,d)\) and \(\mathrm{E} = (v,e)\): \[ \xymatrix{ Z \ar[r]^-{v}_-{e} & Y \ar[r]^-{u}_-{d} & X }. \]

As U runs through the open subsets of X, the composite maps \[ \Gamma(v^{-1}u^{-1}\mathrm{U}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{Z}) \xleftarrow{\ e(u^{-1}\mathrm{U})\ } \Gamma(u^{-1}\mathrm{U}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{Y}) \xleftarrow{\ d(\mathrm{U})\ } \Gamma(\mathrm{U}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X}) \] define an S-deviation of \(uv\) which we shall denote by \(de\); when \(d\) is of order \(\leqslant m\) and \(e\) of order \(\leqslant n\), \(de\) is of order \(\leqslant m+n\). We shall also write \(\mathrm{D} \circ \mathrm{E} = (uv, de)\) and we shall say that \(\mathrm{D} \circ \mathrm{E}\) or DE is the composite S-deviation. When \(d\) is the Algebra homomorphism defining \(u\) (\(\mathrm{D} = u\) with the convention above), we also say that DE is the image of E under \(u\).

The map \((\mathrm{D},\mathrm{E}) \mapsto \mathrm{D} \circ \mathrm{E}\) that we have just defined will henceforth allow us to speak of the category of S-deviations, whose objects are the S-schemes and whose morphisms are the S-deviations.

Suppose for example that Y equals \(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{Z} = \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{Z}[\mathrm{T}]/(\mathrm{T}^2)\) and that \(v\) equals the section defined by the Algebra homomorphism from \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{Z}[\mathrm{T}]/(\mathrm{T}^2)\) to \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{Z}\) that vanishes on the class \(t\) of T modulo \(\mathrm{T}^2\). We may then take for \(e\) the morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{Z}\)-Modules that vanishes on the unit section of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{Z}[\mathrm{T}]/(\mathrm{T}^2)\) and sends \(t\) to the unit section of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{Z}\). If we set \(\mathrm{D} = u\) and \(w = uv\), then \(de\) is simply an S-derivation from \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X}\) to \(w_*(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{Z})\); for every S-morphism \(w : \mathrm{Z} \to \mathrm{X}\) we thus obtain a one-to-one correspondence between the S-derivations from \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X}\) to \(w_*(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{Z})\) and the S-deviations of \(w\) of the form \(u \circ \mathrm{E}\), where \(u\) runs through the morphisms from \(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{Z}\) to X extending \(w\).

If \(d\) is an S-deviation of \(u\), then \(d\) is obviously an S′-deviation of \(u\) for every morphism \(s : \mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{S}'\). On the other hand, let \(t : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{S}\) be a morphism with target S, \(u_\mathrm{T} : \mathrm{Y}_\mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{X}_\mathrm{T}\) the morphism deduced from \(u\) by base change and \(t_\mathrm{Y}\), \(t_\mathrm{X}\) the canonical projections from \(\mathrm{Y}_\mathrm{T}\), \(\mathrm{X}_\mathrm{T}\) to Y, X. There then exists one and only one T-deviation of \(u_\mathrm{T}\), which we shall denote by \(d_\mathrm{T}\) or \(d \times \mathrm{T}\), satisfying the equality \(t_\mathrm{X} d_\mathrm{T} = d\,t_\mathrm{Y}\). If we set \(\mathrm{D} = (u,d)\), we shall also write \(\mathrm{D}_\mathrm{T} = (u_\mathrm{T}, d_\mathrm{T})\) and we shall say that \(d_\mathrm{T}\) and \(\mathrm{D}_\mathrm{T}\) are deduced from \(d\) and D by base change.

For example, let \(u : \mathrm{Y} \to \mathrm{X}\) and \(v : \mathrm{Z} \to \mathrm{T}\) be two S-morphisms, and \(d\) and \(e\) S-deviations of \(u\) and \(v\). We shall denote by \(d \times e\) (the product of \(d\) and \(e\)) the S-deviation of \(u \times v\) equal to \(d_\mathrm{T} \circ e_\mathrm{Y} = e_\mathrm{X} \circ d_\mathrm{Z}\). If we set \(\mathrm{D} = (u,d)\) and \(\mathrm{E} = (v,e)\), we shall also write \(\mathrm{D} \times \mathrm{E} = (u \times v, d \times e)\).

Suppose now that Y equals S; then \(u : \mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{X}\) is a section of \(p : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{S}\), that is, an immersion; we can then give the following interpretation of the S-deviations of \(u\): let \(\mathrm{I}_u\) be the kernel of the homomorphism from \(u^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X})\) to \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\) that defines \(u\), let \(d\) be a morphism of \(p^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S})\)-Modules from \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X}\) to \(u_*(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S})\) and let \(d' : u^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X}) \to \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\) be the morphism canonically associated with \(d\). Then \(d\) is an S-deviation of \(u\) of order \(\leqslant n\) if and only if \(d'\) vanishes on \(\mathrm{I}_u^{n+1}\).

This interpretation may be generalized as follows: let \(u : \mathrm{Y} \to \mathrm{X}\) be an arbitrary S-morphism and \(\Gamma u\) the graph of \(u\), that is, the morphism \(\mathrm{Y} \to \mathrm{Y} \times \mathrm{X}\) with components \(\mathrm{Id}_\mathrm{Y}\) and \(u\). For every S-deviation \(d\) of \(u\) of order \(\leqslant n\), one obtains by composition: \[ \xymatrix{ Y \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{diag.}} & Y\times Y \ar[r]^-{d_Y}_-{u_Y} & Y \times X } \] a Y-deviation of \(\Gamma u\) of order \(\leqslant n\) which we shall denote by \(\Gamma d\) (the graph of \(d\)). One thus obtains a bijection \(d \mapsto \Gamma d\) from the set of S-deviations of \(u\) of order \(\leqslant n\) onto the set of Y-deviations of \(\Gamma u\) of order \(\leqslant n\). The inverse bijection associates with a Y-deviation \(\mathrm{Y} \xrightarrow[\Gamma u]{\ e\ } \mathrm{Y} \times \mathrm{X}\) the composite S-deviation \[ \xymatrix{ Y \ar[r]^-{e}_-{\Gamma u} & Y\times X \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{pr}_2} & X }. \]

Let us call J the kernel of the Algebra homomorphism \[ (\Gamma u)^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Y\times X}) \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_Y \] that defines \(\Gamma u\). Taking the above into account, one then sees that the S-deviations of \(u\) of order \(\leqslant n\) correspond canonically to the morphisms of \(\mathscr{O}_Y\)-Modules from \((\Gamma u)^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Y\times X})\) to \(\mathscr{O}_Y\) that vanish on \(\mathrm{J}^{n+1}\).

Let X be an S-scheme. We call an S-differential operator (resp. an S-differential operator of order \(\leqslant n\)) on X any S-deviation (resp. any S-deviation of order \(\leqslant n\)) of the identity morphism of X. By 1.1, an S-differential operator of order \(\leqslant n\) is thus an endomorphism of the \(p^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S})\)-Module \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X}\) satisfying the equalities \((*_n)\) of 1.1.

We shall denote by \(\mathrm{Dif}^n_{X/S}\) the \(\Gamma(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S})\)-module consisting of the S-differential operators of order \(\leqslant n\), and by \(\mathrm{Dif}_{X/S}\) the one consisting of all S-differential operators. As we saw in 1.2, one can compose the S-deviations of \(\mathrm{Id}_\mathrm{X}\), which equips \(\mathrm{Dif}_{X/S}\) with a \(\Gamma(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S})\)-algebra structure; we shall say that it is the algebra of differential operators of X/S. Similarly, we shall denote by \(\mathscr{D}\mathrm{if}_{X/S}\) the sheaf \(\mathrm{U} \mapsto \mathrm{Dif}_{X\times \mathrm{U}/\mathrm{U}}\), where U runs through the open subsets of S.

As we saw in 1.3, one can interpret the differential operators of X/S by means of the graph of the identity morphism of X, that is, of the diagonal morphism \(\Delta = \Delta_{X/S}\) from X to \(\mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}\). Let us translate the statements of 1.3 into the present context:

Let us equip \(\mathscr{O}_{X\times X}\) with the \(\mathrm{pr}_1^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X})\)-Algebra structure defined by \(\mathrm{pr}_1\), so that \(\Delta^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{X\times X})\) is equipped with an Algebra structure over \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X} = \Delta^{-1}\mathrm{pr}_1^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X})\). Let \(\mathrm{I}_{X/S}\) be the kernel of the homomorphism \[ \Delta_{X/S}^a : \Delta^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{X\times X}) \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_X \] defining \(\Delta\), and \(\mathrm{P}^m_{X/S}\) the \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X}\)-Algebra \(\Delta^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{X\times X})/\mathrm{I}_{X/S}^{m+1}\).

If V is an affine open subset of S and U an affine open subset of X lying over V, the set of sections of \(\mathrm{P}^m_{X/S}\) over U is thus equal to \(\mathrm{A} \otimes_k \mathrm{A}/\mathrm{I}^{m+1}\), where we have set \(k = \Gamma(\mathrm{V}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S})\), \(\mathrm{A} = \Gamma(\mathrm{U}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X})\) and where I is the ideal generated by the elements \(a \otimes 1 - 1 \otimes a\), \(a \in \mathrm{A}\).

This being so, by 1.3 we have a canonical isomorphism \[ j_X : \mathrm{Dif}^m_{X/S} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(\mathrm{P}^m_{X/S}, \mathscr{O}_X) \] which may be defined as follows: if \(d\) belongs to \(\mathrm{Dif}^m_{X/S}\) and if \(c\) is a section of \(\mathrm{P}^m_{X/S}\) over U of the form \(a \otimes b + \mathrm{I}^{m+1}\), then \(j_X(d)(c) = a \cdot d(b)\).

Let \(d\) be a differential operator and \(u\) a section of X over S. We call the value of \(d\) at \(u\) the composite S-deviation \[ \xymatrix{ S \ar[r]^-{u} & X \ar[r]^-{d}_-{\mathrm{id}_X} & X }. \]

By 1.3 and 1.4.1, if \(d\) is a differential operator of order \(\leqslant n\), then \(du\) and \(d\) are canonically associated with morphisms \[ d' : u^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_X)/\mathrm{I}_u^{m+1} \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_S \qquad \text{and} \qquad d'' : \mathrm{P}^m_{X/S} \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_X. \]

It is clear that one can construct \(d'\) from \(d''\) in the following way: the square \[ \xymatrix{ X \simeq S\times X \ar[r]^-{u\times X} \ar[d]_-{p} & X\times X \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{pr}_1} \\ S \ar[r]_-{u} & X } \] is cartesian, which allows us to identify X with \(\mathrm{S} \times_\mathrm{X} (\mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X})\), \(u\) with \(\mathrm{S} \times_\mathrm{X} \Delta\), hence \(u^*(\mathrm{P}^m_{X/S})\) with \(u^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_X)/\mathrm{I}_u^{m+1}\). One thus identifies \(u^*(d'')\) with a morphism \(u^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_X)/\mathrm{I}_u^{m+1} \to \mathscr{O}_S\), which is none other than \(d'\).

Let us set as usual \(\mathrm{I}_S = \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{O}_S[\mathrm{T}]/(\mathrm{T}^2)\). Let \(s : \mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{I}_\mathrm{S}\) be the zero section (II 2.1) and \(\sigma\) the canonical deviation of \(s\) that we defined in 1.2.1: the deviation \(\sigma\) is thus the homomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules that vanishes on the unit section of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}[\mathrm{T}]/(\mathrm{T}^2)\) and sends the class \(t\) of T modulo \(\mathrm{T}^2\) to the unit section of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\).

Let X be an S-scheme. With every \(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{S}\)-automorphism \(u\) of \(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{S} \times \mathrm{X}\) inducing the identity on X is associated by composition a differential operator \(\mathrm{D}_u\) of X: \[ \xymatrix{ X \simeq S\times X \ar[r]^-{\sigma\times X} & I_S\times X \ar[r]^-{u} & I_S \times X \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{pr}_2} & X }. \]

By II 4.11, the map \(u \mapsto \mathrm{D}_u\) is an isomorphism from the Lie algebra of the group functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}\ \mathrm{X}\) onto the Lie algebra of \(p^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S})\)-derivations of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X}\). The inverse isomorphism associates with every derivation D the automorphism of \(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{S} \times \mathrm{X}\) corresponding to the automorphism \(a + bt \mapsto a + (\mathrm{D}a + b)t\) of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}[\mathrm{T}]/(\mathrm{T}^2)\).

pages.title: 2. Invariant differential operators on group schemes

blocks.html: 2. Invariant differential operators on group schemes

Let G be an S-group scheme. We denote by \(\varepsilon\) or \(\varepsilon_\mathrm{G} : \mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{G}\) the unit section of G and by U(G) the \(\Gamma(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S})\)-module of S-deviations of \(\varepsilon_\mathrm{G}\) (or S-deviations of the origin) (cf. 1.1). If \(d\) and \(e\) are two elements of U(G), then \(d \times e\) is an S-deviation of \(\varepsilon \times \varepsilon : \mathrm{S} \simeq \mathrm{S} \times \mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{G} \times \mathrm{G}\). The image of \(d \times e\) under the multiplication morphism \(m : \mathrm{G} \times \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}\) (cf. 1.2) will be called the product of \(d\) and \(e\) and will be denoted \(d \cdot e\). The \(\Gamma(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S})\)-module U(G) is thus equipped with the structure of an associative \(\Gamma(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S})\)-algebra having \(\varepsilon_\mathrm{G}\) as unit element (1.1). We shall say that U(G) is the infinitesimal algebra of G. As T runs through the schemes over S, the infinitesimal algebra \(\mathrm{U}(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{T})\) of the T-group \(\mathrm{G} \times \mathrm{T}\) obviously varies contravariantly in T, so that we may speak of the infinitesimal algebra functor. In particular, as T runs through the open subsets of S, the functor \(\mathrm{T} \mapsto \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{T})\) is a sheaf of algebras which we shall denote by \(\mathscr{U}(\mathrm{G})\) and which we shall call the infinitesimal Algebra.

The algebra U(G) is also a covariant functor in G: if \(u : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\) is a homomorphism of S-groups and \(d\) an S-deviation of \(\varepsilon_\mathrm{G}\), the image of \(d\) under \(u\) is an element \(\mathrm{U}(u)(d) = ud\) of U(H). The map \(\mathrm{U}(u) : \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{G}) \to \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{H})\) thus defined is obviously a homomorphism of \(\Gamma(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S})\)-algebras. One likewise defines a homomorphism \(\mathscr{U}(u)\) from \(\mathscr{U}(\mathrm{G})\) to \(\mathscr{U}(\mathrm{H})\).

Now let \(d\) be an S-deviation of the origin of G and \(d \times \mathrm{G}\) the S-deviation of \(\varepsilon \times \mathrm{G} : \mathrm{G} \simeq \mathrm{S} \times \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G} \times \mathrm{G}\) obtained from \(d\) by base change. The image of \(d \times \mathrm{G}\) under the multiplication morphism \(m : \mathrm{G} \times \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}\) is a differential operator \(d^\mathrm{G}\) of G over S. Moreover, the map \(d \mapsto d^\mathrm{G}\) is obviously \(\Gamma(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S})\)-linear and the "commutative" diagram \[ \xymatrix{ & & G\times G\times G \ar[rr]^-{m\times G} \ar[dl]_-{d\times G\times G} \ar[dr]^-{\varepsilon\times G\times G} & & G\times G \ar[dl]^-{G\times m} \ar[dd]^-{m} & \\ & G\times G \ar[dl]_-{\varepsilon\times G} \ar[dr]^(.4){m} & & G\times G \ar[dl]_(.4){d\times G} \ar[dr]^-{\varepsilon\times G} & & \\ G \ar[rr]_-{e^G}^-{\mathrm{Id}\,G} & & G \ar[rr]_-{d^G}^-{\mathrm{Id}\,G} & & G & } \] shows that \((d \cdot e)^\mathrm{G} = d^\mathrm{G} \cdot e^\mathrm{G}\): indeed, the commutativity of the two lower triangles results from the definition of \(d^\mathrm{G}\) and \(e^\mathrm{G}\); on the other hand, the composite S-deviation of \(e \times \mathrm{G}\), \(d \times \mathrm{G} \times \mathrm{G}\) and \(m \times \mathrm{G}\) coincides with \((d \cdot e) \times \mathrm{G}\); its image under \(m\) is therefore equal to \((d \cdot e)^\mathrm{G}\).

One thus obtains a homomorphism, called right translation, from the \(\Gamma(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S})\)-algebra U(G) to \(\mathrm{Dif}_{G/S}\). If \(\mathscr{D}\mathrm{if}_{G/S}\) denotes the sheaf \(\mathrm{U} \mapsto \mathrm{Dif}_{G\times \mathrm{U}/\mathrm{U}}\) on S (1.4), one likewise defines a "Right translation" from the sheaf \(\mathscr{U}(\mathrm{G})\) to \(\mathscr{D}\mathrm{if}_{G/S}\).

We shall now characterize the differential operators of G over S of the form \(d^\mathrm{G}\): let \(g : \mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{G}\) be a section of the structural morphism of G and \(g_\mathrm{G}\) the right translation of G by \(g\), that is, the composite morphism \[ \xymatrix{ G \simeq G\times S \ar[r]^-{G\times g} & G\times G \ar[r]^-{m} & G }. \]

For every differential operator D of G over S, we then denote by \(\mathrm{D}^g\) the operator \(g_\mathrm{G}^{-1}\mathrm{D}g_\mathrm{G}\) (1.2). We say that D is right invariant if, for every base change \(t : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{S}\) and every section \(g : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{G} \times \mathrm{T}\), we have \((\mathrm{D}_\mathrm{T})^g = \mathrm{D}_\mathrm{T}\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma

For every differential operator D of G over S, the following assertions are equivalent:

(i) D is right invariant.

(ii) If \(m\) is the multiplication morphism of G, we have \(\mathrm{D}m = m(\mathrm{D} \times \mathrm{G})\).

(ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i): since condition (ii) is stable under base change, it suffices to show that (ii) implies the equality \(\mathrm{D}^g = \mathrm{D}\) for every section \(g : \mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{G}\). This follows from the commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ G \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{D}}^-{\mathrm{Id}\,G} & G\times G \ar[l]_-{m} \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{D}\times G}^-{\mathrm{Id}(G\times G)} & G \ar[l]_-{h} \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{D}}_-{\mathrm{Id}\,G} \\ G & G\times G \ar[l]^-{m} & G \ar[l]^-{h} } \] where \(h\) is the morphism \[ \xymatrix{ G \ar[r]^-{\sim} & G\times S \ar[r]^-{G\times g} & G\times G } \] and \(m \circ h\) is the right translation by \(g\).

(i) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii): indeed, take for \(t : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{S}\) the structural morphism \(p : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{S}\), and for the section \(g : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{G} \times \mathrm{T}\) the diagonal morphism \(\Delta : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G} \times \mathrm{G}\). The right translation of \(\mathrm{G} \times \mathrm{G}\) by \(\Delta\) is then the morphism from \(\mathrm{G} \times \mathrm{G}\) to \(\mathrm{G} \times \mathrm{G}\) whose components are \(m\) and \(\mathrm{pr}_2\). The equality \((\mathrm{D}_\mathrm{G})^\Delta = \mathrm{D}_\mathrm{G}\) is then equivalent to the commutativity of the first square of the following diagram \[ \xymatrix{ G\times G \ar[r]^-{\Delta_{G\times G}} \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{D}_G}^-{\mathrm{Id}\,G\times G} & G\times G \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{pr}_1} \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{D}_G}^-{\mathrm{Id}\,G\times G} & G \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{D}}_-{\mathrm{Id}\,G} \\ G\times G \ar[r]_-{\Delta_{G\times G}} & G\times G \ar[r]_-{\mathrm{pr}_1} & G } \] Equality (ii) therefore results from the fact that \(m = \mathrm{pr}_1 \circ \Delta_{G\times G}\).

Consider for example an element \(d\) of the infinitesimal algebra U(G). The two squares of the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ G\times G \ar[r]^-{d\times G\times G}_-{\varepsilon\times G\times G} \ar[d]_-{m} & G\times G\times G \ar[r]^-{m\times G} \ar[d]^-{G\times m} & G\times G \ar[d]^-{m} \\ G \ar[r]_-{d\times G}^-{\varepsilon\times G} & G\times G \ar[r]_-{m} & G } \] are then commutative. Since \(m \circ (d\times \mathrm{G}) = d^\mathrm{G}\) and \((m\times \mathrm{G}) \circ (d\times \mathrm{G}\times \mathrm{G}) = d^\mathrm{G}\times \mathrm{G}\), we also have \(d^\mathrm{G} \circ m = m \circ (d^\mathrm{G} \times \mathrm{G})\). For every S-deviation \(d\) of the origin, \(d^\mathrm{G}\) is therefore a right invariant differential operator.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.4

The map \(d \mapsto d^\mathrm{G}\) is an isomorphism from the infinitesimal algebra U(G) onto the subalgebra \(\mathrm{Dif}^\mathrm{G}_{G/S}\) of \(\mathrm{Dif}_{G/S}\) consisting of the right invariant differential operators.

Indeed, let D be an arbitrary differential operator of G over S and denote by \(\mathrm{D}_0\) its value at the origin, that is, the composite deviation \(\mathrm{S} \xrightarrow{\ \varepsilon\ } \mathrm{G} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{id}_G]{\ \mathrm{D}\ } \mathrm{G}\). The right invariant differential operator \((\mathrm{D}_0)^\mathrm{G}\) is then obtained by composition: \[ \xymatrix{ G \simeq S\times G \ar[r]^-{\varepsilon\times G} & G\times G \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{D}\times G}_-{\mathrm{id}_{G\times G}} & G\times G \ar[r]^-{m} & G }. \]

If D is right invariant, we have \(\mathrm{D}m = m(\mathrm{D} \times \mathrm{G})\), whence \(\mathrm{D} = \mathrm{D}m(\varepsilon \times \mathrm{G}) = m(\mathrm{D} \times \mathrm{G})(\varepsilon \times \mathrm{G}) = \mathrm{D}_0^\mathrm{G}\). In particular, the map \(d \mapsto d^\mathrm{G}\) is surjective.

Conversely, let \(d\) be an S-deviation of the origin. We then have a commutative square \[ \xymatrix{ G\times G & G \ar[l]_-{d\times G} \\ G\times S \simeq G \ar[u]^-{G\times\varepsilon} & S \ar[l]^-{d} \ar[u]_-{\varepsilon} } \] from which it follows that \(d = m(\mathrm{G} \times \varepsilon)d = m(d\times \mathrm{G})\varepsilon = (d^\mathrm{G})_0\). A fortiori, the map \(d \mapsto d^\mathrm{G}\) is injective.

As S varies, Theorem 2.4 obviously implies that the Right translation \(\mathscr{U}(\mathrm{G}) \to \mathscr{D}\mathrm{if}_{G/S}\) is an isomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Algebras from \(\mathscr{U}(\mathrm{G})\) onto the sub-Algebra \(\mathscr{D}\mathrm{if}^\mathrm{G}_{G/S} : \mathrm{U} \mapsto \mathrm{Dif}^{G_U}_{G_U/U}\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.4.1

Consider the commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ G \ar@<0.5ex>[d]^-{p} & G\times G \ar[l]_-{\eta} \ar@<0.5ex>[d]^-{\mathrm{pr}_1} \\ S \ar@<0.5ex>[u]^-{\varepsilon} & G \ar[l]^-{p} \ar@<0.5ex>[u]^-{\Delta} }\ , \] where \(\eta\) denotes the morphism "\((x,y) \mapsto yx^{-1}\)". The latter induces morphisms \[ \eta' : \eta^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_G) \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_{G\times G} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \Delta^{-1}(\eta') : p^{-1}\varepsilon^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_G) \longrightarrow \Delta^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{G\times G}). \]

For every integer \(n \geqslant 1\), \(\Delta^{-1}(\eta')\) defines, by passage to the quotient, a homomorphism of sheaves: \[ \eta^n : p^{-1}(p^n_{G/S}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{P}^n_{G/S}, \] where we have set \(p^n_{G/S} = \varepsilon^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_G)/\mathrm{I}_\varepsilon^{n+1}\) (see 1.3 and 1.4 for the notation). Since the square formed by the morphisms \(\eta\), \(p\), \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) and \(p\) is cartesian, \(\eta^n\) induces an isomorphism from \(p^*(p^n_{G/S})\) onto \(\mathrm{P}^m_{G/S}\).

The differential operators of G over S of order \(\leqslant n\) therefore correspond one-to-one to the morphisms of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}\)-Modules \(p^*(p^n_{G/S}) \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}\), that is, to the morphisms of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules \[ p^n_{G/S} \longrightarrow p_*(\mathscr{O}_G). \] Under this bijection, the right invariant differential operators are associated with the composite arrows \[ \xymatrix{ p^n_{G/S} \ar[r] & \mathscr{O}_S \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{can.}} & p_*(\mathscr{O}_G) }. \] One thus recovers the isomorphism of Theorem 2.4.

Let \(\gamma : \mathrm{G} \to \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}\ \mathrm{G}\) be the homomorphism of group functors that associates with an S-morphism \(g : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{G}\) the left translation of \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{T}\) by \(g\), that is, the composite morphism \[ \xymatrix{ G_T \simeq T\times_T G_T \ar[r]^-{g\times G_T} & G_T\times_T G_T \ar[r]^-{m_T} & G_T }. \]

This homomorphism \(\gamma\) defines the diagram of sets below \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Lie}\,G \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{Lie}\,\gamma} & \mathrm{Lie}(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}\,G) \ar[d]^-{\beta} \\ U(G) \ar[r]_-{\delta} & \mathrm{Dif}_{G/S} } \] where \(\beta\) denotes the map \(u \mapsto \mathrm{D}_u\) of 1.5, and \(\delta\) the right translation defined in 2.2. If \(x\) is an element of \(\mathrm{Lie}\,\mathrm{G}\), that is, a morphism from \(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{S}\) to G such that \(xs = \varepsilon_\mathrm{G}\) with the notation of 1.5, we have the following commutative square which determines the image of \(x\) under \(\mathrm{Lie}\,\gamma\): \[ \xymatrix{ I_S\times G \ar[r]^-{(\mathrm{Lie}\,\gamma)(x)} \ar[d]_-{x\times G} & I_S\times G \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{pr}_2} \\ G\times G \ar[r]_-{m} & G }. \]

By 1.5, the image of \((\mathrm{Lie}\,\gamma)(x)\) under \(\beta\) is the composite deviation \[ \xymatrix{ G \simeq S\times G \ar[r]^-{\sigma\times G}_-{\mathrm{Id}} & I_S\times G \ar[r]^-{x\times G} & G\times G \ar[r]^-{m} & G }. \] By 2.2, this composite deviation is none other than \((x\sigma)^\mathrm{G}\). Denoting by \(\alpha\) the map \(x \mapsto x\sigma\) from \(\mathrm{Lie}\,\mathrm{G}\) to U(G), we therefore have \[ \beta(\mathrm{Lie}\,\gamma) = \delta\alpha. \]
In particular, \(\alpha\) is an injective homomorphism from \(\mathrm{Lie}\,\mathrm{G}\) into the Lie algebra underlying the infinitesimal algebra U(G).
3. Coalgebras and Cartier duality

Let S be a scheme (or, more generally, a ringed space). An \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Coalgebra is a pair \((\mathscr{U}, \Delta_\mathscr{U})\) consisting of an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module \(\mathscr{U}\) and a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules \(\Delta_\mathscr{U} : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{U} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{U}\) (called the diagonal morphism) such that:
(i) \(\sigma \circ \Delta_\mathscr{U} = \Delta_\mathscr{U}\), where \(\sigma(a\otimes b) = b\otimes a\).

(ii) The square \[ \xymatrix{ U \ar[r]^-{\Delta_U} \ar[d]_-{\Delta_U} & U\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} U \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{id}_U\otimes\Delta_U} \\ U\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} U \ar[r]_-{\Delta_U\otimes\mathrm{id}_U} & U\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} U\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} U } \] is commutative.

(iii) There exists a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules \(\varepsilon_\mathscr{U} : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{O}_S\), called the augmentation, such that the composite morphisms \[ \xymatrix{ U \ar[r]^-{\Delta_U} & U\otimes\mathscr{O}_S U \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{id}_U\otimes\varepsilon_U} & U\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_S \simeq U } \] \[ \xymatrix{ U \ar[r]^-{\Delta_U} & U\otimes\mathscr{O}_S U \ar[r]^-{\varepsilon_U\otimes\mathrm{id}_U} & \mathscr{O}_S\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} U \simeq U } \] are the identity morphism of \(\mathscr{U}\).

If \(\varepsilon_\mathscr{U}\) and \(\varepsilon'_\mathscr{U}\) are two augmentations, we have \(\varepsilon_\mathscr{U} \simeq (\varepsilon_\mathscr{U}\otimes\varepsilon'_\mathscr{U})\circ\Delta_\mathscr{U} \simeq \varepsilon'_\mathscr{U}\); the augmentation is therefore uniquely determined by (iii).

If \((\mathscr{U},\Delta_\mathscr{U})\) and \((\mathscr{V},\Delta_\mathscr{V})\) are two \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebras, a morphism from the first to the second is a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules \(f : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{V}\) such that the diagrams \[ \xymatrix{ U \ar[r]^-{f} \ar[d]_-{\Delta_U} & V \ar[d]^-{\Delta_V} \\ U\otimes U \ar[r]_-{f\otimes f} & V\otimes V } \qquad \text{and} \qquad \xymatrix{ U \ar[rr]^-{f} \ar[dr]_-{\varepsilon_U} & & V \ar[dl]^-{\varepsilon_V} \\ & \mathscr{O}_S & } \] are commutative. Morphisms of Coalgebras compose like morphisms of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules, so that we may speak of the category of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebras.

This category has finite products: the final object is the \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\), the diagonal morphism being the identity; the product of two Coalgebras \((\mathscr{U},\Delta_\mathscr{U})\) and \((\mathscr{V},\Delta_\mathscr{V})\) is the tensor product \(\mathscr{U} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{V}\), the diagonal morphism being the composite morphism \[ \xymatrix{ U\otimes V \ar[r]^-{\Delta_U\otimes\Delta_V} & U\otimes U\otimes V\otimes V \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{id}_U\otimes\sigma\otimes\mathrm{id}_V} & U\otimes V\otimes U\otimes V } \] where \(\sigma(a\otimes b) = b\otimes a\); the canonical projections of \(\mathscr{U} \otimes \mathscr{V}\) onto the factors \(\mathscr{U}\) and \(\mathscr{V}\) are the morphisms \(\mathrm{id}_U\otimes\varepsilon_V\) and \(\varepsilon_U\otimes\mathrm{id}_V\).

Let \(\mathscr{A}\) be a commutative \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Algebra, locally free and of finite type as an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module. If we set \[ \mathscr{A}^* = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-Mod.}}(\mathscr{A}, \mathscr{O}_S), \] the canonical morphism \(\varphi\) from \(\mathscr{A}^* \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{A}^*\) to \((\mathscr{A} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{A})^*\) is invertible. If \(m : \mathscr{A} \otimes \mathscr{A} \to \mathscr{A}\) is the morphism defining the multiplication of \(\mathscr{A}\), one obtains by composition a diagonal morphism \[ \xymatrix{ \Delta_{\mathscr{A}^*} : \mathscr{A}^* \ar[r]^-{m^*} & (\mathscr{A}\otimes\mathscr{A})^* \ar[r]^-{\varphi^{-1}} & \mathscr{A}^*\otimes\mathscr{A}^* }. \]

This diagonal morphism obviously makes \(\mathscr{A}^*\) an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebra whose augmentation is the transpose of the morphism \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S} \to \mathscr{A}\) defined by the unit section of \(\mathscr{A}\). Moreover, it is clear that the functor \(\mathscr{A} \mapsto \mathscr{A}^*\) is an antiequivalence from the category of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Algebras that are locally free and of finite type as \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules, to the category of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebras that are locally free and of finite type as \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules.

With every \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebra \(\mathscr{U}\) is canonically associated an S-functor \(\mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U} : (\mathbf{Sch}/\mathrm{S})^\circ \to (\mathbf{Ens})\): indeed, note that, for every S-scheme \(q : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{S}\), \(q^*(\mathscr{U} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{U})\) is identified with \(q^*(\mathscr{U}) \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_T} q^*(\mathscr{U})\), so that \(q^*(\Delta_\mathscr{U})\) makes \(\mathscr{U}_\mathrm{T} = q^*(\mathscr{U})\) an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{T}\)-Coalgebra; we may therefore set by definition, with an obvious abuse of notation: \[ (\mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U})(\mathrm{T}) = \{x \in \Gamma(\mathrm{T},\mathscr{U}_T) \mid \varepsilon_{\mathscr{U}_T}(x) = 1 \ \text{and} \ \Delta_{\mathscr{U}_T}(x) = x\otimes x\}. \]

The sections \(x\) of \(\mathscr{U}_\mathrm{T}\) obviously correspond to the morphisms of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{T}\)-Modules \(\xi : \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{T} \to \mathscr{U}_\mathrm{T}\); the conditions \(\varepsilon(x) = 1\) and \(\Delta(x) = x\otimes x\) simply express that \(\xi\) is a morphism of Coalgebras. We therefore also have: \[ (\mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U})(\mathrm{T}) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_T\text{-coalg.}}(\mathscr{O}_T, \mathscr{U}_T). \]

In particular, if \(\mathscr{A}\) is a commutative \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Algebra that is locally free of finite type as an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module, we have isomorphisms \[ (\mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{A}^*)(\mathrm{T}) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_T\text{-coalg.}}(\mathscr{O}_T, \mathscr{A}^*_T) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_T\text{-alg.}}(\mathscr{A}_T, \mathscr{O}_T) \simeq (\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathrm{A})(\mathrm{T}) \] and \[ \mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{A}^* \simeq \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{A}. \]

An \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebra in groups, that is, a group in the category of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebras, consists of the data of an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebra \((\mathscr{U},\Delta_\mathscr{U})\) and a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebras \(m_\mathscr{U} : \mathscr{U} \otimes \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{U}\). Such a morphism is a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules making the following diagrams commutative

(iv) \[ \xymatrix{ U\otimes U\otimes U\otimes U \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{id}_U\otimes\sigma\otimes\mathrm{id}_U} & U\otimes U \ar[l]_-{\Delta_U\otimes\Delta_U} \ar[ddr]^-{m_U} \\ U\otimes U\otimes U\otimes U \ar[r]_-{m_U\otimes m_U} & U\otimes U \ar[dr]_-{\Delta_U} \\ & & U } \]
(v) \[ \xymatrix{ U\otimes U \ar[rr]^-{m_U} \ar[dr]_-{\varepsilon_U\otimes\varepsilon_U} & & U \ar[dl]^-{\varepsilon_U} \\ & \mathscr{O}_S & } \]
The morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebras \(m_\mathscr{U}\) must in addition satisfy the conditions (ii)\(^*\), (iii)\(^*\) and (vi) below:

(ii)\(^*\) The square \[ \xymatrix{ U\otimes U\otimes U \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{id}_U\otimes m_U} \ar[d]_-{m_U\otimes\mathrm{id}_U} & U\otimes U \ar[d]^-{m_U} \\ U\otimes U \ar[r]_-{m_U} & U } \] is commutative.

(iii)\(^*\) There exists a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebras \(\eta_\mathscr{U} : \mathscr{O}_S \to \mathscr{U}\) such that the composite morphisms \[ \xymatrix{ U \simeq U\otimes\mathscr{O}_S \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{id}_U\otimes\eta_U} & U\otimes U \ar[r]^-{m_U} & U } \] \[ \text{and} \qquad \xymatrix{ U \simeq \mathscr{O}_S\otimes U \ar[r]^-{\eta_U\otimes\mathrm{id}_U} & U\otimes U \ar[r]^-{m_U} & U } \] are the identity morphisms of \(\mathscr{U}\).

(vi) There exists a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebras \(c_\mathscr{U} : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{U}\) such that the composite morphism \[ \xymatrix{ U \ar[r]^-{\Delta_U} & U\otimes U \ar[r]^-{c_U\otimes\mathrm{id}_U} & U\otimes U \ar[r]^-{m_U} & U } \] equals \(\eta_\mathscr{U} \circ \varepsilon_\mathscr{U}\).

The morphisms \(\eta_\mathscr{U}\) and \(c_\mathscr{U}\) of (iii)\(^*\) and (vi) are obviously unique. The conditions (ii)\(^*\) and (iii)\(^*\) simply express that \(m_\mathscr{U}\) makes \(\mathscr{U}\) an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-algebra whose unit section is the image under \(\eta_\mathscr{U}\) of the unit section of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\). Condition (iv) also expresses that the diagonal morphism \(\Delta_\mathscr{U}\) is compatible with the multiplication; and indeed, \(\Delta_\mathscr{U} : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{U} \otimes \mathscr{U}\) must be a homomorphism of Coalgebras in groups, which also implies the commutativity of the triangle
(v)\(^*\) \[ \xymatrix{ & \mathscr{O}_S \ar[dl]_-{\eta_U} \ar[dr]^-{\eta_U\otimes\eta_U} & \\ U \ar[rr]_-{\Delta_U} & & U\otimes U }\ . \]

On the other hand, as in any category, the antipodism \(c_\mathscr{U}\) is an isomorphism from \(\mathscr{U}\) onto the opposite Coalgebra in groups; in particular, \(c_\mathscr{U}\) induces an isomorphism of algebras from \(\mathscr{U}\) onto the opposite algebra \(\mathscr{U}^\circ\).

Since the functor \(\mathscr{U} \mapsto \mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U}\) commutes with finite products, it transforms a Coalgebra in groups into an S-group functor; and indeed, for every S-scheme T, the elements \(x \in \Gamma(\mathrm{T}, \mathscr{U}_T)\) belonging to \((\mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U})(\mathrm{T})\) form a group under the multiplication of the algebra \(\Gamma(\mathrm{T}, \mathscr{U}_T)\); the inverse of \(x\) is none other than \(c_\mathscr{U}(x)\).

For example, let \(\mathfrak{g}\) be an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Lie Algebra and \(\mathscr{U}(\mathfrak{g})\) the enveloping Algebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\), that is, the sheaf on S associated with the presheaf assigning to every open subset V the enveloping algebra \(\mathrm{U}(\Gamma(\mathrm{V},\mathfrak{g}))\) of the Lie algebra \(\Gamma(\mathrm{V},\mathfrak{g})\).

Every homomorphism from \(\mathfrak{g}\) into the Lie Algebra underlying an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Algebra factors in one and only one way through the canonical morphism from \(\mathfrak{g}\) to \(\mathscr{U}(\mathfrak{g})\); moreover, this universal property implies, besides the functoriality of \(\mathscr{U}(\mathfrak{g})\) in \(\mathfrak{g}\), that the enveloping Algebra of a product of Lie Algebras is identified with the tensor product of the enveloping Algebras.

In particular, the diagonal morphism \(\delta : \mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{g} \times \mathfrak{g}\) induces a homomorphism of Algebras \(\Delta : \mathscr{U}(\mathfrak{g}) \to \mathscr{U}(\mathfrak{g}\times\mathfrak{g}) \simeq \mathscr{U}(\mathfrak{g}) \otimes \mathscr{U}(\mathfrak{g})\). The zero morphism \(\mathfrak{g} \to 0\) induces a homomorphism \(\varepsilon : \mathscr{U}(\mathfrak{g}) \to \mathscr{U}(0) \simeq \mathscr{O}_S\). The isomorphism \(x \mapsto -x\) from \(\mathfrak{g}\) onto the opposite Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}^\circ\) induces an antiisomorphism \(c\) of the algebra \(\mathscr{U}(\mathfrak{g})\). One then easily checks that the multiplication \(m\) of the Algebra \(\mathscr{U}(\mathfrak{g})\) makes \((\mathscr{U}(\mathfrak{g}),\Delta)\) an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebra in groups having \(\varepsilon\) as augmentation and \(c\) as antipodism.

Let \(\mathscr{U}\) be an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebra in groups. One easily checks that \(\mathrm{G} = \mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U}\) is a good S-group (II 4.6). Since we have \[ \Gamma(\mathrm{I}_S, \mathscr{U}_{I_S}) \simeq \Gamma(\mathrm{S},\mathscr{U}) \oplus d\,\Gamma(\mathrm{S},\mathscr{U}), \] an element \(u_0+du_1\) of \(\Gamma(\mathrm{I}_S,\mathscr{U}_{I_S})\) belongs to \((\mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U})(\mathrm{I}_S)\) if and only if we have \[ \Delta(u_0+du_1) = (u_0+du_1)\otimes(u_0+du_1) \qquad \text{and} \qquad \varepsilon(u_0+du_1) = 1, \] whence \(\Delta u_0 + d\Delta u_1 = u_0\otimes u_0 + (u_1\otimes u_0 + u_0\otimes u_1)d\) and \(\varepsilon(u_0)+d\varepsilon(u_1) = 1\), that is \[ \Delta u_0 = u_0\otimes u_0, \qquad u_1 = u_1\otimes u_0 + u_0\otimes u_1 \qquad \text{and} \qquad \varepsilon(u_0)=1,\ \varepsilon(u_1)=0. \]

In particular, \((\mathrm{Lie}\,\mathrm{G})(\mathrm{T})\) is the set of primitive elements of \(\Gamma(\mathrm{T},\mathscr{U}_T)\), that is, of the elements \(u\) such that \(\Delta u = u\otimes 1 + 1\otimes u\) (with the obvious abuse of notation already mentioned).

The \(\Gamma(\mathrm{T},\mathscr{O}_T)\)-module structure of \((\mathrm{Lie}\,\mathrm{G})(\mathrm{T})\) is obviously induced by that of \(\Gamma(\mathrm{T},\mathscr{U}_T)\). On the other hand, consider two primitive elements \(u\) and \(v\) of \((\mathrm{Lie}\,\mathrm{G})(\mathrm{S})\) and set \(\mathrm{I} = \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{O}_S[d]/(d^2)\) and \(\mathrm{I}' = \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{O}_S[d']/(d'^2)\). Since the composition law of \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{I}\times \mathrm{I}')\) is induced by the multiplication of the Algebra \(\mathscr{U}_{I\times I'}\), we have \[ (1+ud)(1+vd')(1+ud)^{-1}(1+vd')^{-1} = (1+ud)(1+vd')(1-ud)(1-vd) \] \[ = 1 + (uv-vu)dd' \] Whence the equality \([u,v] = uv-vu\), which proves that G is very good (II 4.10).
Suppose finally that \(\mathscr{U}\) is an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebra in commutative groups, that is, that the triangle
(i)\(^*\) \[ \xymatrix{ U\otimes U \ar[rr]^-{\sigma} \ar[dr]_-{m_U} & & U\otimes U \ar[dl]^-{m_U} \\ & U & } \]

is commutative, or equivalently that \(m_\mathscr{U}\) makes \(\mathscr{U}\) a commutative \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Algebra. The conditions (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v), (vi), (i)\(^*\), (ii)\(^*\), (iii)\(^*\) and (v)\(^*\) then also mean that \(\mathscr{U}\) is a cogroup in the category of commutative \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Algebras. In particular, if moreover \(\mathscr{U}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module, \(\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{U}\) is a commutative S-group scheme.

A homomorphism of S-groups from \(\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{U}\) to \(\mathbb{G}_{m,S}\) (I 4.3.2) is then induced by a homomorphism of unital \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Algebras \[ \varphi : \mathscr{O}_S[\mathrm{T},\mathrm{T}^{-1}] \longrightarrow \mathscr{U} \] such that \((\varphi\otimes\varphi)\circ\Delta' = \Delta_U\circ\varphi\) (the diagonal morphism \(\Delta'\) of \(\mathscr{O}_S[\mathrm{T},\mathrm{T}^{-1}]\) sends T to \(\mathrm{T}\otimes\mathrm{T}\)). Such a homomorphism \(\varphi\) is determined by the image \(\varphi(\mathrm{T})\), which must be an invertible element \(x\) of \(\mathscr{U}\) such that \(\Delta_\mathscr{U} x = x\otimes x\); since \(\varphi\) then necessarily commutes with the augmentation, we have \(\varepsilon_\mathscr{U} x = 1\) (the augmentation of \(\mathscr{O}_S[\mathrm{T},\mathrm{T}^{-1}]\) sends T to 1). We therefore have \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{U}, \mathbb{G}_{m,S}) \simeq (\mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U})(\mathrm{S}). \] Since this formula remains valid after any base change, we finally have \[ \mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U} = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{U}, \mathbb{G}_m) \] for every quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebra in commutative groups \(\mathscr{U}\).

If we suppose moreover that \(\mathscr{U}\) is a locally free \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module of finite type, \(\mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U}\) is also representable and we have (cf. 3.1.2): \[ \mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U} \simeq \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{U}^*. \] The functor \(\mathscr{U} \mapsto \mathscr{U}^* = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-Mod.}}(\mathscr{U},\mathscr{O}_S)\) therefore induces a duality(*) of the category of commutative S-group schemes that are finite locally free over S (this is Cartier duality). By 3.3, this duality associates \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{G},\mathbb{G}_{m,S})\) with an S-group G.

(*) A duality of a category \(\mathscr{C}\) is a pair \((\mathrm{D},\varphi)\) consisting of a contravariant functor D from \(\mathscr{C}\) to \(\mathscr{C}\) and a functorial isomorphism \(\varphi : \mathrm{Id}_{\mathscr{C}} \to \mathrm{DD}\) such that the isomorphisms \(\varphi\mathrm{D} : \mathrm{D} \to \mathrm{DDD}\) and \(\mathrm{D}\varphi : \mathrm{DDD} \to \mathrm{D}\) are inverse to each other.
4. « Frobeniuseries »

Let \(p\) be a fixed prime number and \((\mathbf{Sch}/\mathbb{F}_p)\) the category of schemes of characteristic \(p\), that is, of schemes over the prime field \(\mathbb{F}_p\). Following the general conventions of this seminar, we identify \((\mathbf{Sch}/\mathbb{F}_p)\) with a subcategory of \(\widehat{(\mathbf{Sch}/\mathbb{F}_p)}\) by means of the functor \(\mathbf{h}\) of I 1.1. We likewise take advantage of the isomorphism from \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbf{h}_X, \mathrm{F})\) onto \(\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{X})\) defined in I 1.1 to identify these two sets whenever X is an \(\mathbb{F}_p\)-scheme and F an object of \(\widehat{(\mathbf{Sch}/\mathbb{F}_p)}\). If T is an \(\mathbb{F}_p\)-scheme, a T-functor is a morphism \(q : \mathrm{F} \to \mathrm{T}\) of \(\widehat{(\mathbf{Sch}/\mathbb{F}_p)}\) having T as target; for every T-scheme \(r : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{T}\), the set of morphisms \(s : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{F}\) such that \(q\circ s = r\) will then be denoted \(q(r)\), \(q(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{T})\), \(\mathrm{F}(r)\) or \(\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{T})\) (or even \(\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{X})\) when no confusion is possible with \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbf{h}_X, \mathrm{F})\)).

For every scheme S of characteristic \(p\), we denote by \(\mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{S})\) or \(\mathrm{fr}\) the endomorphism of S that induces the identity on the topological space underlying S and associates \(x^p\) with a section \(x\) of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\) over an open subset U. Then the map \(\mathrm{fr} : \mathrm{S} \mapsto \mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{S})\) is an endomorphism of the identity functor of \((\mathbf{Sch}/\mathbb{F}_p)\), which implies the following results: let E be an \(\mathbb{F}_p\)-functor, that is, an object of \(\widehat{(\mathbf{Sch}/\mathbb{F}_p)}\); the map that associates with every \(\mathbb{F}_p\)-scheme S the endomorphism \(\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{S}))\) of \(\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{S})\) is a functorial endomorphism of E which we shall denote by \(\mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{E})\) or \(\mathrm{fr}\); this notation is compatible with the identification of \((\mathbf{Sch}/\mathbb{F}_p)\) with a subcategory of \(\widehat{(\mathbf{Sch}/\mathbb{F}_p)}\); moreover, the map \(\mathrm{E} \mapsto \mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{E})\) is an endomorphism of the identity functor of \(\widehat{(\mathbf{Sch}/\mathbb{F}_p)}\) (which we shall again denote by fr).

For every scheme S of characteristic \(p\) and every S-functor \(q : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{S}\), we denote by \(\mathrm{X}^{(p/S)}\) or \(\mathrm{X}^{(p)}\) the inverse image \(\mathrm{S} \times_{\mathrm{fr},q} \mathrm{X}\) of X under the base change \(\mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{S})\). The commutative square \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{fr}(X)} \ar[d]_-{q} & X \ar[d]^-{q} \\ S \ar[r]_-{\mathrm{fr}(S)} & S } \] then induces an S-morphism \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{S})\) or Fr from X to \(\mathrm{X}^{(p/S)}\) such that \(\mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{X}) = \mathrm{pr}_2 \circ \mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{S})\). We shall say that \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{S})\) is the Frobenius morphism of X relative to S; it is clear that the map \(\mathrm{Fr} : \mathrm{X} \mapsto \mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{S})\) is a functorial homomorphism.

By the definitions, for every S-scheme \(\mathrm{K} : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{S}\), the map \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{K})\) may therefore be characterized by the commutativity of the triangle
(†) \[ \xymatrix{ & X^{(p/S)}(K) \ar[dr]^-{\sim} & \\ X(K) \ar[ur]^-{\mathrm{Fr}(X/S)(K)} \ar[rr]_-{X(\mathrm{fr}(T))} & & X(\mathrm{fr}(S)\circ K) = X(K\circ \mathrm{fr}(T)) }\ . \]

For example, if X is the subscheme of S defined by a quasi-coherent Ideal \(\mathscr{I}\), then \(\mathrm{X}^{(p)}\) is the subscheme of S defined by the Ideal \(\mathscr{I}^{\{p\}}\) generated by the \(p\)-th powers of the sections of \(\mathscr{I}\); moreover, \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{S})\) is then the canonical immersion of \(\mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{I})\) into \(\mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}/\mathscr{I}^{\{p\}})\).

Let us now consider a base change \(t : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{S}\). Since the square \[ \xymatrix{ T \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{fr}(T)} \ar[d]_-{t} & T \ar[d]^-{t} \\ S \ar[r]_-{\mathrm{fr}(S)} & S } \] is commutative, the inverse image of \(\mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{T}\) under \(\mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{T})\) is identified with the inverse image of \(\mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{S}\) under \(t\); in other words, we have a canonical isomorphism from \(\mathrm{X}_T^{(p/T)}\) onto \((\mathrm{X}^{(p/S)})_T\). It is clear that, under this identification, \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{X}_T/\mathrm{T})\) is identified with the inverse image \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{S})_T\) of \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{S})\).

In particular, if S is the spectrum of the prime field \(\mathbb{F}_p\), \(\mathrm{X}^{(p/S)}\) equals X and \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{S})\) equals \(\mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{X})\). Consequently, \(\mathrm{X}_T^{(p/T)}\) is identified with \(\mathrm{X}_T\) and \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{X}_T/\mathrm{T})\) with \(\mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{X})_T\). For example, let E be a set and \(\mathrm{E}_\mathrm{T}\) the constant T-scheme of type E; we then have \(\mathrm{E}_T^{(p/T)} \simeq \mathrm{E}_T\) and \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{E}_T/\mathrm{T}) \simeq \mathrm{Id}_{E_T}\).

The functor \(\mathrm{X} \mapsto \mathrm{X}^{(p/S)}\) obviously commutes with products; it therefore transforms an S-group G into an S-group \(\mathrm{G}^{(p/S)}\); moreover, since Fr is a functorial homomorphism, \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) is a homomorphism of S-groups. We shall denote by \(\mathrm{Fr}\,\mathrm{G}\) the kernel of this homomorphism: if \(q : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{S}\) is a scheme over S, it follows from 4.1 that the value of \(\mathrm{Fr}\,\mathrm{G}\) at \(q\) is the kernel of the homomorphism \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{T})) : \mathrm{G}(q) \to \mathrm{G}(q\circ\mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{T}))\). For example, when T is the scheme \(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{R}\) of dual numbers over an S-scheme R, \(\mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{I}_R)\) factors as follows \[ \xymatrix{ I_R \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{can.}} & R \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{fr}(R)} & R \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{can.}} & I_R }. \] This shows that \((\mathrm{Fr}\,\mathrm{G})(\mathrm{I}_R)\) contains the kernel of the morphism \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{I}_R) \to \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{R})\) and that \(\mathrm{Lie}\ \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S} = \mathrm{Lie}(\mathrm{Fr}\,\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\).

More generally, for every S-functor X, we define the S-functor \(\mathrm{X}^{(p^n)}\) by induction on \(n\) using the formulas \[ \mathrm{X}^{(p)} = \mathrm{X}^{(p/S)} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \mathrm{X}^{(p^n)} = (\mathrm{X}^{(p^{n-1})})^{(p)}. \] Likewise, \(\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{S})\) or \(\mathrm{Fr}^n\) denote the composite functorial homomorphism \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{Fr}(X/S)} & X^{(p)} \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{Fr}(X^{(p)}/S)} & X^{(p^2)} \ar[r] & \cdots \ar[r] & X^{(p^{n-1})} \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{Fr}(X^{(p^{n-1})}/S)} & X^{(p^n)} }. \]

If G is an S-group functor, so is \(\mathrm{G}^{(p^n)}\), and \(\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) is a homomorphism of S-group functors. We shall denote by \(\mathrm{Fr}_n\,\mathrm{G}\) the kernel of \(\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) and we shall say that G is of height \(\leqslant n\) if \(\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) is zero, that is, if \(\mathrm{Fr}_n\,\mathrm{G} = \mathrm{G}\).

The subgroup functor \(\mathrm{Fr}_n\,\mathrm{G}\) of G is characteristic, in the sense that, for every S-scheme T, every endomorphism \(f\) of the T-group functor \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{T}\) induces an endomorphism of \((\mathrm{Fr}_n\,\mathrm{G})_T\): indeed, since the construction of \(\mathrm{G}^{(p^n)}\) and of \(\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) commutes with base change by 4.1.1, we may assume \(\mathrm{T} = \mathrm{S}\); in this case, the assertion follows from the fact that \(\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) is a functorial homomorphism. Finally, note that \(\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{X}^{(p)}/\mathrm{S})\) coincides with \(\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{S})^{(p)}\).
Here are a few examples:

a) Consider first an "abstract" abelian group M and the diagonalizable group \(\mathrm{G} = \mathbf{D}_S(\mathrm{M})\) of type M (I 4.4): for every S-scheme T, \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T})\) is thus the abelian group \(\mathrm{Hom}_{(Ab)}(\mathrm{M}, \Gamma(\mathrm{T},\mathscr{O}_T)^\times)\). Since G is the inverse image of the diagonalizable group \(\mathbf{D}(\mathrm{M})\) over \(\mathbb{F}_p\), \(\mathrm{G}^{(p)}\) is identified with G and \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{T})\) is identified with the endomorphism \(x \mapsto x^p\) of \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T})\) (4.1.1). In particular, when M equals \(\mathbb{Z}\), we have \(\mathbf{D}_S(\mathrm{M}) = \mathbb{G}_{m,S}\), so that \(\mathrm{Fr}\,\mathbb{G}_{m,S}\) is the S-group \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{p,S}\) that associates with every S-scheme T the group of \(p\)-th roots of unity of \(\Gamma(\mathrm{T},\mathscr{O}_T)\).

b) Consider now a scheme S of characteristic \(p\) and a sheaf of modules E on S. By I 4.6.2, we have a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathbf{W}(E)^{(p)} \simeq \mathbf{W}(E^{(p)}), \] where \(\mathrm{E}^{(p)}\) is the inverse image of E under \(\mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{S})\). Moreover, by 4.1, the map \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathbf{W}(E))(q)\) is determined for every S-scheme T by the commutative triangle \[ \xymatrix{ \Gamma(T, q^*\mathrm{fr}(S)_*E) \ar[rr]^-{\sim}_-{\mathrm{can.}} & & \Gamma(T,\mathrm{fr}(T)_*q^*E) \\ & \Gamma(T,q^*E) \ar[ul]^-{\mathrm{Fr}(\mathbf{W}(E)/S)(q)} \ar[ur]_-{f'} & }\ , \] where \(f'\) is the map induced by \(\mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{T})\).

In particular, if E equals \(\mathscr{O}_S\), \(\mathbf{W}(E)\) is identified with the additive group \(\mathbb{G}_{a,S}\). In this case, we have \(\mathrm{E}^{(p)} = \mathrm{E} = \mathscr{O}_S\) and the Frobenius morphism \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathbb{G}_{a,S}/\mathrm{S})\) sends \(x \in \Gamma(\mathrm{T},\mathscr{O}_T)\) to \(x^p\). The kernel \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{p,S}\) of the Frobenius morphism of \(\mathbb{G}_{a,S}\) therefore associates with every S-scheme T the set of sections \(x\) of \(\mathscr{O}_T\) such that \(x^p = 0\).

c) One would see likewise that, for every quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Algebra \(\mathscr{A}\), \((\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{A})^{(p)}\) is identified with the spectrum \(\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{A}^{(p)}\) of the inverse image of \(\mathscr{A}\) under \(\mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{S})\). If \(\pi\) denotes the endomorphism \(x \mapsto x^p\) of the sheaf of rings \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\), we thus have \(\mathscr{A}^{(p)} = \mathscr{A} \otimes_\pi \mathscr{O}_S\) and it is clear that \(\mathrm{Fr}((\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{A})/\mathrm{S})\) is induced by the homomorphism \(a \otimes_\pi x \mapsto a^p x\) from \(\mathscr{A} \otimes_\pi \mathscr{O}_S\) to \(\mathscr{A}\).

Finally, for every quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module E, we have canonical isomorphisms \[ \mathbb{V}(E)^{(p)} \simeq \mathbb{V}(E^{(p)}) \qquad \text{and} \qquad \mathscr{S}(E)^{(p)} \simeq \mathscr{S}(E^{(p)}), \] where \(\mathscr{S}(E)\) denotes the symmetric Algebra of the \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module E.

d) Let \(\mathscr{U}\) be an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebra (3.1) and T a scheme of characteristic \(p\). If \(\mathscr{U}^{(p/S)}\) or \(\mathscr{U}^{(p)}\) denote the inverse image of the Coalgebra \(\mathscr{U}\) under \(\mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{S})\), we have, as in b), a canonical isomorphism: \[ (\mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U})^{(p)} \simeq \mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U}^{(p)}. \]

If \(\mathscr{U}\) is a Coalgebra in groups, the value of \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U})\) for an S-scheme T is therefore the set of elements \(\gamma\) of \(\Gamma(\mathrm{T},\mathscr{U}_T)\) such that \[ \varepsilon_{\mathscr{U}_T}(x) = 1, \qquad \Delta_{\mathscr{U}_T}x = x\otimes x, \qquad \text{and} \qquad \gamma\otimes_{\mathrm{fr}(T)} 1 = 1. \]

We shall now occupy ourselves with a construction akin to the preceding one: let S be a scheme of characteristic \(p\), X an S-scheme and \(\mathrm{X}^p_S\) the product, in the category \((\mathbf{Sch}/\mathrm{S})\), of \(p\) copies of X. We then denote by \(\mathrm{U}^p(\mathrm{X})\) the open subscheme of \(\mathrm{X}^p_S\) that is the union of the products \(\mathrm{U}^p_S\), as U runs through the affine open subsets of X. A point \(x\) of \(\mathrm{X}^p_S\) therefore belongs to \(\mathrm{U}^p(\mathrm{X})\) if and only if the projections \(\mathrm{pr}_i\,x\) of \(x\) onto the factors of \(\mathrm{X}^p_S\) belong to one and the same affine open subset of X. For example, if every finite subset of X is contained in an affine open subset, we have \(\mathrm{U}^p(\mathrm{X}) = \mathrm{X}^p_S\).

The symmetric group \(\mathfrak{S}_p\) of order \(p\) acts on \(\mathrm{X}^p_S\) by permutation of the factors and leaves the open subset \(\mathrm{U}^p(\mathrm{X})\) stable. We shall call the \(p\)-fold symmetric product of X, and denote by \(\Sigma^p\mathrm{X}\), the quotient of \(\mathrm{X}^p_S\) by \(\mathfrak{S}_p\) in the category of all ringed spaces. The canonical projection \(q : \mathrm{X}^p_S \to \Sigma^p\mathrm{X}\) maps \(\mathrm{U}^p(\mathrm{X})\) onto an open subset \(\mathrm{V}^p(\mathrm{X})\) of the symmetric product, which may be described as follows (see V 4.1): the structure sheaf of \(\Sigma^p\mathrm{X}\) induces on \(\mathrm{V}^p(\mathrm{X})\) a scheme structure; the morphism \(q' : \mathrm{U}^p(\mathrm{X}) \to \mathrm{V}^p(\mathrm{X})\) induced by \(q\) is affine and even integral; as U runs through the affine open subsets of X that project into a variable affine open subset V of S, the \(\Sigma^p\mathrm{U}\) form an affine cover of \(\mathrm{V}^p(\mathrm{X})\); if \(k\) denotes the affine algebra of V and A that of U, \(\Sigma^p\mathrm{U}\) has as affine algebra the subalgebra \(\Sigma^p\mathrm{A}\) of \(\bigotimes^p_k \mathrm{A}\) consisting of the symmetric tensors.

Let us now consider the diagonal morphism \(\delta\) from X to \(\mathrm{U}^p(\mathrm{X})\). The restriction of \(\delta\) to the open subset U above is defined by the algebra homomorphism \(\eta : a_1\otimes\cdots\otimes a_p \mapsto a_1\cdot a_2\cdots a_p\) from \(\bigotimes^p_k \mathrm{A}\) to A. We therefore have, if N is the symmetrization operator, \[ \eta(\mathrm{N}(a_1\otimes\cdots\otimes a_p)) = \eta\Big(\sum_{\sigma\in\mathfrak{S}_p} a_{\sigma(1)}\otimes\cdots\otimes a_{\sigma(p)}\Big) = p!\ a_1\cdots a_p = 0. \]

In other words, \(\eta\) vanishes on the subspace \(\mathrm{N}(\bigotimes^p_k \mathrm{A})\) of \(\Sigma^p\mathrm{A}\) consisting of the symmetrized tensors. Moreover, if \(f\) is a symmetric tensor, we obviously have \(\mathrm{N}(fa) = f\mathrm{N}(a)\), which shows that \(\mathrm{N}(\bigotimes^p_k \mathrm{A})\) is an ideal of \(\Sigma^p\mathrm{A}\).

We shall from now on denote by \(\mathrm{U}^{[p/S]}\) the prime spectrum of the algebra \(\Sigma^p\mathrm{A}/\mathrm{N}(\otimes^p_k\mathrm{A})\). This spectrum is a closed subscheme of \(\Sigma^p(\mathrm{U}) = \mathrm{V}^p(\mathrm{U})\) and the union of the \(\mathrm{U}^{[p/S]}\) is a closed subscheme \(\mathrm{X}^{[p/S]}\) of \(\mathrm{V}^p(\mathrm{X})\).
Moreover, if \(i(\mathrm{X})\) denotes the inclusion of \(\mathrm{X}^{[p/S]}\) in \(\mathrm{V}^p(\mathrm{X})\), we have just seen that \(q' \circ \delta\) factors through \(\mathrm{X}^{[p/S]}\):
\[ \xymatrix{ X^p_S & \supset & U^p(X) \ar[d]^-{q'(X)} & X \ar[l]_-{\delta(X)} \ar[d]^-{F^{[p]}(X/S)} \\ & & \Sigma^p(X) \supset V^p(X) & X^{[p/S]} \ar[l]^-{i(X)} }\ . \]
It is clear that \(\mathrm{X}^{[p/S]}\) is functorial in X and that the map \(\mathrm{F}^{[p]} : \mathrm{X} \mapsto \mathrm{F}^{[p]}(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{S})\) is a functorial homomorphism.

The schemes \(\mathrm{X}^{[p/S]}\) and \(\mathrm{X}^{(p/S)}\) are obviously related: let V be an affine open subset of S with affine ring \(k\) and U an affine open subset of X over V; let A be the affine algebra of U. If \(\pi\) denotes the endomorphism \(x \mapsto x^p\) of \(k\), then \(\mathrm{X}^{(p/S)}\) has \(k_\pi \otimes \mathrm{A}\) as affine algebra. One checks moreover that the map \(\lambda\otimes a \mapsto (\lambda a\otimes\cdots\otimes a \mod \mathrm{N}(\otimes^p_k\mathrm{A}))\) defines a homomorphism of \(k\)-algebras from \(\mathrm{A}\otimes_\pi k\) to \(\Sigma^p\mathrm{A}/\mathrm{N}(\otimes^p_k \mathrm{A})\); this homomorphism induces a morphism \(\varphi(\mathrm{U}) : \mathrm{U}^{[p/S]} \to \mathrm{U}^{(p/S)}\) such that \(\varphi(\mathrm{U})\circ \mathrm{F}^{[p]}(\mathrm{U}/\mathrm{S}) = \mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{U}/\mathrm{S})\).
"Gluing the pieces together", one then obtains a commutative triangle \[ \xymatrix{ & X \ar[dl]_-{F^{[p]}(X/S)} \ar[dr]^-{\mathrm{Fr}(X/S)} & \\ X^{[p/S]} \ar[rr]_-{\varphi(X)} & & X^{(p/S)} }\ . \]

For example, if X is the subscheme of S defined by a quasi-coherent Ideal \(\mathscr{I}\), \(\mathrm{F}^{[p]}(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{S})\) is identified with the identity morphism of X, so that \(\varphi(\mathrm{X})\) is the canonical immersion of \(\mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_S/\mathscr{I})\) into \(\mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_S/\mathscr{I}^{\{p\}})\). One thus sees that \(\varphi(\mathrm{X})\) is not an isomorphism in general. However, when M is a free \(k\)-module, it is clear that the map \(\lambda\otimes m \mapsto (\lambda m\otimes\cdots\otimes m \pmod{\mathrm{N}(\otimes^p_k \mathrm{M})})\) from \(k_\pi \otimes \mathrm{M}\) to \(\Sigma^p\mathrm{M}/\mathrm{N}(\otimes^p_k \mathrm{M})\) is bijective; this map therefore remains bijective when M is \(k\)-flat, because every flat module is a filtered inductive limit of free modules (Lazard(*)). It follows that \(\varphi(\mathrm{X}) : \mathrm{X}^{[p/S]} \to \mathrm{X}^{(p/S)}\) is an isomorphism when X is a flat S-scheme.
(*) D. Lazard, C.R. Acad. Sc. Paris 258, 1964, p. 6313-6316.

Finally, consider an S-scheme in abelian groups G. Then the composite morphism \(\mu(\mathrm{G}) : \mathrm{U}^p(\mathrm{G}) \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{incl.}\ } \mathrm{G}^p_S \to \mathrm{G}\), defined by the multiplication, factors through \(\mathrm{V}^p(\mathrm{G})\), so that we have the following commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ G & U^p(G) \ar[l]_-{\mu(G)} \ar[d]_-{q'(G)} \ar[r]^-{\delta(G)} & G \ar[dl]^-{F^{[p]}(G/S)} \ar@/^3pc/[dd]^-{\mathrm{Fr}(G/S)} \\ V^p(G) \ar[u]_-{\nu(G)} \ar[r]_-{i(G)} & G^{[p/S]} \ar[dr]_-{\varphi(G)} & \\ & & G^{(p/S)} \ar@{-->}@/^3pc/[uull]_-{\mathrm{Ver}(G/S)} }\ . \]

When G is S-flat, \(\varphi(\mathrm{G})\) is an isomorphism and one can define a morphism (called the Verschiebung) \[ \mathrm{Ver}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}) : \ \mathrm{G}^{(p/S)} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] by means of the formula \(\mathrm{Ver}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}) = \nu(\mathrm{G})\circ i(\mathrm{G})\circ\varphi(\mathrm{G})^{-1}\). As G runs through the flat S-schemes in abelian groups, the map \(\mathrm{Ver} : \mathrm{G} \mapsto \mathrm{Ver}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) is obviously a functorial homomorphism; consequently, \(\mathrm{Ver}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) is a homomorphism of groups. Finally, for every S-scheme T, the composite map \[ \xymatrix{ G(T) \ar[r]^-{\delta(G)(T)} & U^p(G)(T) \ar[r]^-{\mu(G)(T)} & G(T) } \] sends \(x \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T})\) to \(p \cdot x\). We may write \(p \cdot \mathrm{id}_G\) instead of \(\mu(\mathrm{G})\circ\delta(\mathrm{G})\), thus obtaining the classical formula: \[ \mathrm{Ver}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}) \circ \mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}) = p \cdot \mathrm{id}_G. \tag{\(*\)} \]

For example, when G is a constant S-scheme in abelian groups, we know that \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) is identified with the identity morphism of G (4.1.1). We therefore have \(\mathrm{Ver}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}) = p\).

When G is the diagonalizable S-group of type M, \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) equals \(p\) by 4.1.2; one easily sees that \(\mathrm{Ver}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) is the identity morphism of G.

When E is a flat \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module and G is the S-group \(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E})\), the morphism \(\mathrm{Ver}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) is zero, as is \(p \cdot \mathrm{id}_G\). It will be seen in Exposé VII B that a commutative algebraic group G over a field \(k\) is "unipotent" if and only if the composite homomorphism \[ \xymatrix{ G^{(p^n)} \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{Ver}(G^{(p^{n-1})}/S)} & G^{(p^{n-1})} \ar[r] & \cdots \ar[r] & G^{(p)} \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{Ver}(G/S)} & G } \] is zero for some \(n\) (we have set \(\mathrm{G}^{(p^n)} = (\mathrm{G}^{(p^{n-1})})^{(p)}\)).

Since the map \(\mathrm{Ver} : \mathrm{G} \mapsto \mathrm{Ver}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) is a functorial homomorphism as G runs through the flat S-schemes in commutative groups, the square \[ \xymatrix{ G^{(p)} \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{Ver}(G/S)} \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{Fr}(G/S)^{(p)}} & G \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{Fr}(G/S)} \\ G^{(p^2)} \ar[r]_-{\mathrm{Ver}(G^{(p)}/S)} & G^{(p)} } \] is commutative (where \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})^{(p)}\) denotes the inverse image of \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) under the base change \(\mathrm{fr}(\mathrm{S})\)). Since it follows directly from the definitions that \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})^{(p)}\) equals \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{G}^{(p)}/\mathrm{S})\), we also have \[ \mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}) \circ \mathrm{Ver}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}) = \mathrm{Ver}(\mathrm{G}^{(p)}/\mathrm{S}) \circ \mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{G}^{(p)}/\mathrm{S}) = p \cdot \mathrm{id}_{G^{(p)}}. \tag{\(**\)} \]

Suppose finally that G is a commutative S-group, finite and locally free; let A be the affine \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Algebra of G and \(\pi\) the endomorphism of the sheaf of rings \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\) sending a section \(x\) of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\) to \(x^p\). We then have a commutative diagram

\((\mathscr{A})\) \[ \xymatrix{ & \mathscr{A} \ar[rr]^-{\Delta^p(\mathscr{A})} \ar[dl]_-{a(\mathscr{A})} & & \displaystyle\bigotimes^p_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{A} \ar[rr]^-{m^p(\mathscr{A})} \ar[dr]^-{q(\mathscr{A})} & & \mathscr{A} \\ \Sigma^p\mathscr{A} \ar[urrr]^(.3){i(\mathscr{A})} \ar[dr]_-{r(\mathscr{A})} & & & & \mathscr{S}^p(\mathscr{A}) \ar[ul]_(.3){b(\mathscr{A})} \ar[dl]^-{j(\mathscr{A})} & \\ & & \mathscr{A}\otimes_\pi \mathscr{O}_S & & }\ . \]

where the various letters have the following meanings: \(m^p(\mathrm{A})\) is induced by the multiplication of A; the morphism \(\Delta^p(\mathrm{A})\) is associated with the morphism \(\mu(\mathrm{G})\) discussed above (it is thus defined by the diagonal morphism of the Coalgebra A); we denote by \(\Sigma^p\mathrm{A}\) the sub-Algebra of \(\bigotimes^p_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathrm{A}\) consisting of the sections invariant under the action of the symmetric group, and by \(i(\mathrm{A})\) the inclusion of \(\Sigma^p\mathrm{A}\) in the tensor product; likewise, \(\mathscr{S}^p(\mathrm{A})\) is the component of degree \(p\) of the symmetric algebra of A and \(q(\mathrm{A})\) is the canonical projection. Since \(\Sigma^p\mathrm{A}\) is the affine algebra of \(\mathrm{V}^p(\mathrm{A})\) with the notation above, the composite morphism \(i(\mathrm{G})\circ\varphi(\mathrm{G})^{-1}\) induces a homomorphism of Algebras \(r(\mathrm{A})\); this homomorphism vanishes on the sections of the form \[ \sum_{\sigma\in\mathfrak{S}_p} a_{\sigma(1)}\otimes\cdots\otimes a_{\sigma(p)} \] and sends \(x\otimes\cdots\otimes x\) to \(1\otimes x\). Likewise, \(j(\mathrm{A})\) is the morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules \(1\otimes x \mapsto q(x\otimes\cdots\otimes x)\). The composite \(r(\mathrm{A})\circ a(\mathrm{A})\) is thus associated with the Verschiebung morphism \(\mathrm{Ver}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\), while \(b(\mathrm{A})\circ j(\mathrm{A})\) is associated with the Frobenius morphism \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) (\(a(\mathrm{A})\) and \(b(\mathrm{A})\) are such that \(i(\mathrm{A})\circ a(\mathrm{A}) = \Delta^p(\mathrm{A})\) and \(b(\mathrm{A})\circ q(\mathrm{A}) = m^p(\mathrm{A})\)).

The commutative diagram \((\mathscr{A})\) above is self-dual; indeed, let D be the functor associating with every \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module M the dual \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathrm{M},\mathscr{O}_S)\); it is clear that the image of \((\mathscr{A})\) under the functor D is none other than the diagram \((\mathrm{D}\mathscr{A})\), the morphisms \(\mathrm{D}r(\mathrm{A})\), \(\mathrm{D}a(\mathrm{A})\), \(\mathrm{D}j(\mathrm{A})\) and \(\mathrm{D}b(\mathrm{A})\) being identified respectively with \(j(\mathrm{D}\mathscr{A})\), \(b(\mathrm{D}\mathscr{A})\), \(r(\mathrm{D}\mathscr{A})\) and \(a(\mathrm{D}\mathscr{A})\). One thus sees that Cartier duality (3.3.1) exchanges the Frobenius morphism and the Verschiebung.
5. p-Lie algebras
Let us first recall some results from the Séminaire Sophus Lie:

Let \(p\) be a prime number, R a commutative ring of characteristic \(p\) and A an associative, but not necessarily commutative, R-algebra. If \(a\) and \(b\) are two elements of A, we set \([a,b] = ab-ba\) and \(ab = \mathrm{L}_a(b) = \mathrm{R}_b(a)\). We then have: \[ (\mathrm{ad}\,x^p)(y) = [x^p,y] = (\mathrm{L}_x^p - \mathrm{R}_x^p)(y) = (\mathrm{L}_x-\mathrm{R}_x)^p(y) = (\mathrm{ad}\,x)^p(y) \] whence the first Jacobson formula \[ \mathrm{ad}(x^p) = (\mathrm{ad}\,x)^p \tag{i} \] Likewise, if \(a_1,\ldots,a_p\) are \(p\) arbitrary elements of A, we have (see 4.2): \[ \mathrm{N}(a_1\otimes\cdots\otimes a_p) = \sum_\sigma a_{\sigma(1)}\cdots a_{\sigma(p)} = \sum_\tau [a_{\tau(1)}[a_{\tau(2)}[\cdots[a_{\tau(p-1)},a_p]\cdots]]] \tag{\(*\)} \] where \(\sigma\) runs through the permutations of \(p\) letters and \(\tau\) through those of \((p-1)\) letters. Indeed, the second member equals \[ \sum_\tau \sum_{r=0}^{p-1} (-1)^s\, a_{\tau(i_1)} a_{\tau(i_2)}\cdots a_{\tau(i_r)}\, a_p\, a_{\tau(j_s)}\cdots a_{\tau(j_1)} \] where \(\tau\) runs through the permutations of \(p-1\) letters, \(i_1,\ldots,i_r\) through the strictly increasing sequences of integers in the interval \([1,p-1]\), and where \(j_1,\ldots,j_s\) denotes the strictly increasing sequence whose values are the integers of \([1,p-1]\) different from \(i_1,\ldots,i_r\). For a fixed value of \(r\), the sum of the terms \((-1)^s a_{\tau(i_1)}\cdots a_{\tau(i_r)}\, a_p\, a_{\tau(j_s)}\cdots a_{\tau(j_1)}\) obviously equals \[ (-1)^s \binom{p-1}{s} \sum_\rho a_{\rho(1)}\cdots a_{\rho(r)}\, a_p\, a_{\rho(r+1)}\cdots a_{\rho(p-1)} \] where \(\rho\) runs through the permutations of \(p-1\) letters. The equalities \[ (x-1)^p = x^p-1 = (x-1)(x^{p-1}+\cdots+1) \qquad \text{and} \qquad (x-1)^{p-1} = x^{p-1}+\cdots+1 \] show on the other hand that \((-1)^s\binom{p-1}{s}\) equals 1 in characteristic \(p\), which proves \((*)\).

In particular, if \(x_0\) and \(x_1\) are two elements of A, we have \[ (x_0+x_1)^p = x_0^p + x_1^p + \sum x_{z(1)}x_{z(2)}\cdots x_{z(p)}, \] where \(z\) runs through the non-constant maps from \([1,p]\) to \(\{0,1\}\). From this one deduces \[ (x_0+x_1)^p = x_0^p + x_1^p + \sum_{0<r<p} \frac{1}{r!(p-r)!}\, \mathrm{N}(x_0,x_0,\ldots,x_0,x_1,\ldots,x_1), \] whence the second Jacobson formula \[ (x_0+x_1)^p = x_0^p + x_1^p - \sum_{0<r<p} \sum_t \frac{1}{r}\big[x_{t(1)}\big[x_{t(2)}\big[\cdots\big[x_{t(p-1)},x_1\big]\cdots\big]\big]\big] \tag{ii} \] where \(t\) runs through the maps \([1,p-1] \to \{0,1\}\) taking the value 0 \(r\) times.

Now let \(\mathfrak{g}\) be an R-Lie algebra. One says that a map \(x \mapsto x^{(p)}\) from \(\mathfrak{g}\) to \(\mathfrak{g}\) makes \(\mathfrak{g}\) a p-Lie algebra over R if the following conditions are satisfied:
(0) \((\lambda x)^{(p)} = \lambda^p \cdot x^{(p)}\), \(\lambda \in \mathrm{R}\), \(x \in \mathfrak{g}\)
(i) \(\mathrm{ad}\,x^{(p)} = (\mathrm{ad}\,x)^p\), \(x \in \mathfrak{g}\)
(ii) \((x_0+x_1)^{(p)} = x_0^{(p)} + x_1^{(p)} - \displaystyle\sum_{0<r<p}\sum_t \frac{1}{r}\big[x_{t(1)}\big[x_{t(2)}\big[\cdots\big[x_{t(p-1)},x_1\big]\cdots\big]\big]\big]\)
where \(t\) runs through the maps \([1,p-1] \to \{0,1\}\) taking the value 0 \(r\) times (\(x_0,x_1\in\mathfrak{g}\)).

For example, if A is an R-algebra, we saw in 5.1 that one obtains a \(p\)-Lie algebra \(\mathrm{A}_\mathfrak{g}\) by taking the R-module underlying A and setting \[ [x,y] = xy-yx\ , \qquad x^{(p)} = x^p\ , \qquad x,y \in \mathrm{A} \] We shall say that \(\mathrm{A}_\mathfrak{g}\) is the \(p\)-Lie algebra underlying A. In what follows we shall mostly consider \(p\)-Lie subalgebras of \(p\)-algebras of the form \(\mathrm{A}_\mathfrak{g}\); here is an example: let S be a scheme of characteristic \(p > 0\) and X an S-scheme. Recall that a derivation of X over S is an endomorphism D of the sheaf of abelian groups \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X}\) such that \[ \mathrm{D}(\lambda\cdot s) = \lambda\cdot \mathrm{D}(s) \qquad \text{and} \qquad \mathrm{D}(s\cdot t) = (\mathrm{D}s)t + s(\mathrm{D}t) \] as \(\lambda\) runs through the sections of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\), and \(s\) and \(t\) through the sections of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X}\) over open subsets such that the formulas make sense. The Leibniz formula \[ \mathrm{D}^n(s\cdot t) = \sum_{i=0}^n \binom{n}{i} (\mathrm{D}^i s)(\mathrm{D}^{n-i}t) \] shows that \(\mathrm{D}^p\) is again a derivation of X over S, in view of the equality \(\binom{p}{i} \equiv 0 \pmod p\) for \(i \neq 0, p\). It follows that the Algebra \(\mathrm{Dér}_{X/S}\) of derivations of X over S is a p-Lie subalgebra of the algebra over \(\Gamma(\mathrm{S},\mathscr{O}_S)\) of differential operators of X over S.

If \(\mathfrak{g}\) and \(\mathfrak{h}\) are two \(p\)-Lie algebras, a homomorphism \(h : \mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{h}\) is an R-linear map from \(\mathfrak{g}\) to \(\mathfrak{h}\) such that \(h([x,y]) = [h(x),h(y)]\) and \(h(x^{(p)}) = h(x)^{(p)}\) for \(x,y \in \mathfrak{g}\). The composite of two homomorphisms is again a homomorphism, so that we may speak of the category of \(p\)-Lie algebras over R. Likewise, if \((\mathrm{X},\mathscr{R})\) is a ringed space, we shall say that an \(\mathscr{R}\)-Module \(\mathfrak{g}\) is equipped with a structure of \(p\)-Lie Algebra over \(\mathscr{R}\) if, for every open subset U, \(\Gamma(\mathrm{U},\mathfrak{g})\) is equipped with a structure of \(p\)-Lie algebra over \(\Gamma(\mathrm{U},\mathscr{R})\) and the restrictions are homomorphisms.

We are now interested in the left adjoint functor of the functor \(\mathrm{A} \mapsto \mathrm{A}_\mathfrak{g}\) of 5.2: let \(\mathfrak{g}\) be a \(p\)-Lie algebra over the ring R of characteristic \(p\), \(\mathrm{U}(\mathfrak{g})\) the enveloping algebra of the Lie algebra underlying \(\mathfrak{g}\) (cf. Bourbaki, Lie algebras, §2) and \(i_\mathfrak{g}\) or \(i : \mathfrak{g} \to \mathrm{U}(\mathfrak{g})\) the canonical map. One knows that every homomorphism of Lie algebras \(h\) from \(\mathfrak{g}\) into the Lie algebra underlying a unital R-algebra A extends in one and only one way to a homomorphism of unital R-algebras \(g\) from \(\mathrm{U}(\mathfrak{g})\) to A (\(gi = h\)). Moreover, \(h\) is a homomorphism of \(p\)-Lie algebras if and only if \(g\) vanishes on the elements \(i(x)^p - i(x^{(p)})\), as \(x\) runs through \(\mathfrak{g}\). Consequently, if \(\mathrm{U}_p^R(\mathfrak{g})\) or \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\) denotes the quotient of \(\mathrm{U}(\mathfrak{g})\) by the two-sided ideal generated by the elements \(i(x)^p - i(x^{(p)})\), and if \(j_\mathfrak{g}\) or \(j : \mathfrak{g} \to \mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\) is the composite of \(i : \mathfrak{g} \to \mathrm{U}(\mathfrak{g})\) and the canonical map from \(\mathrm{U}(\mathfrak{g})\) to \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\), one sees that every homomorphism of \(p\)-Lie algebras from \(\mathfrak{g}\) to \(\mathrm{A}_\mathfrak{g}\) extends in one and only one way to a homomorphism of unital algebras \(g : \mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g}) \to \mathrm{A}\) (\(gj = h\)).
One says that \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\) is the restricted enveloping algebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

With the notation of 5.3, let us now set \(\underline{p}(x) = i(x)^p - i(x^{(p)})\). For every element \(y\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\), we have \[ \underline{p}(x)i(y) = i(y)\underline{p}(x) + [\underline{p}(x),i(y)] = i(y)\underline{p}(x) + i((\mathrm{ad}\,x)^p y) - (\mathrm{ad}\,i(x))^p(i(y)) = i(y)\underline{p}(x), \] so that \(\underline{p}(x)\) belongs to the center of \(\mathrm{U}(\mathfrak{g})\); in particular, the left ideal generated by the elements \(\underline{p}(x)\) is already two-sided. Moreover, we have \(\underline{p}(\lambda x) = \lambda^p \underline{p}(x)\) and \(\underline{p}(x+y) = \underline{p}(x)+\underline{p}(y)\), for \(\lambda \in \mathrm{R}\) and \(x,y \in \mathfrak{g}\); in particular, if \((x_\alpha)\) is a family of generators of the R-module \(\mathfrak{g}\), the left ideal generated by the elements \(\underline{p}(x)\) is already generated by the \(\underline{p}(x_\alpha)\).

When \(\mathfrak{g}\) is an R-Lie algebra whose underlying R-module is free with basis \((x_\alpha)\), one can define a \(p\)-Lie algebra structure on \(\mathfrak{g}\) as follows: let us identify \(\mathfrak{g}\) with a submodule of \(\mathrm{U}(\mathfrak{g})\) by means of \(i\) (Bourbaki, Lie Alg., I, §2.7) and let \(\pi\) be the map \(r \mapsto r^p\) from R to R. If \((y_\alpha)\) is an arbitrary family of elements of \(\mathfrak{g}\) such that \(\mathrm{ad}\,y_\alpha = (\mathrm{ad}\,x_\alpha)^p\), there then exists an R-linear map \(\underline{q}\) from \(\mathrm{R}\otimes_\pi \mathfrak{g}\) to \(\mathrm{U}(\mathfrak{g})\) sending \(1\otimes x_\alpha\) to \(x_\alpha^p - y_\alpha\); moreover, since we have \[ (\mathrm{ad}\,x_\alpha^p)(x) = (\mathrm{ad}\,x_\alpha)^p(x) = (\mathrm{ad}\,y_\alpha)(x) \] for every \(x \in \mathfrak{g}\), \(\underline{q}\) maps \(\mathfrak{g}^{(p)}\) into the center of \(\mathrm{U}(\mathfrak{g})\).

Setting then \(x^{\{p\}} = x^p - \underline{q}(1\otimes x)\) for every \(x \in \mathfrak{g}\), one checks without difficulty that \(x^{\{p\}}\) belongs to \(\mathfrak{g}\) and that the map \(x \mapsto x^{\{p\}}\) makes \(\mathfrak{g}\) a \(p\)-Lie algebra. In other words, if \((x_\alpha)\) is a basis of the module underlying a Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}\) over R, the p-Lie algebra structures on \(\mathfrak{g}\) correspond one-to-one to the families \((y_\alpha)\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\) such that \(\mathrm{ad}\,y_\alpha = (\mathrm{ad}\,x_\alpha)^p\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.3.3

Let \(\mathfrak{g}\) be a \(p\)-Lie algebra over R whose underlying module is free with basis \((x_\alpha)\). Then the map \(j : \mathfrak{g} \to \mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\) is injective and, if one sets \(z_\alpha = j(x_\alpha)\), \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\) has as basis the monomials \(\prod_\alpha z^{n_\alpha}\) (\(0 \leqslant n_\alpha < p\); the \(n_\alpha\) are assumed to be zero except for finitely many of them; the basis is assumed to be totally ordered and the products are carried out in increasing order).

Indeed, let \(n = (n_\alpha)\) be a family of natural integers that are zero except for finitely many of them. Let us identify \(\mathfrak{g}\) with a submodule of the enveloping algebra \(\mathrm{U}(\mathfrak{g})\) by means of the canonical map \(i\); set \(|n| = \sum_\alpha n_\alpha\), \(x^n = \prod_\alpha x^{n_\alpha}\) and let \(\mathrm{U}^r\) be the submodule of \(\mathrm{U}(\mathfrak{g})\) generated by the \(x^n\) with \(|n| \leqslant r\). Finally set \(s = |n|\), \(n_\alpha = m_\alpha + p\ell_\alpha\), with \(0 \leqslant m_\alpha < p\), and \(\mathrm{T}n = \prod_\alpha x^{m_\alpha}\underline{p}(x_\alpha)^{\ell_\alpha}\) (see 5.3.1).
