It is clear that \(\mathrm{T}n - \prod_\alpha x^{n_\alpha}\) belongs to \(\mathrm{U}^{s-1}\). By the Poincaré-Birkhoff-Witt theorem, the \(\mathrm{T}n\) with \(|n| = s\) therefore form a basis of \(\mathrm{U}^s\) modulo \(\mathrm{U}^{s-1}\). As \(s = |n|\) varies, the \(\mathrm{T}n\) form a basis of \(\mathrm{U}(\mathfrak{g})\). Now the kernel J of the canonical map from \(\mathrm{U}(\mathfrak{g})\) to \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\) is the left ideal of \(\mathrm{U}(\mathfrak{g})\) generated by the central elements \(\underline{p}(x_\alpha)\) (5.3.1); consequently, the \(\mathrm{T}n\) with \(\ell = (\ell_\alpha) \neq 0\) form a basis of J; the \(\mathrm{T}n\) with \(n_\alpha < p\) for all \(\alpha\) form a basis of \(\mathrm{U}(\mathfrak{g})\) modulo J, q.e.d.

Let \(\mathfrak{g}\) be a \(p\)-Lie algebra over R and \(f : \mathrm{R} \to \mathrm{R}'\) an extension of the base ring. I claim that there exists on the R′-module \(\mathrm{R}' \otimes_\mathrm{R} \mathfrak{g}\) one and only one \(p\)-Lie algebra structure such that \[ [\lambda\otimes x, \mu\otimes y] = \lambda\mu\otimes[x,y] \qquad \text{and} \qquad (\lambda\otimes x)^{(p)} = \lambda^p\otimes x^{(p)}. \]
It will follow in particular that the functor \(\mathfrak{g} \mapsto \mathrm{R}'\otimes_\mathrm{R}\mathfrak{g}\) is left adjoint to the functor "restriction of scalars from R′ to R".

The uniqueness of the \(p\)-Lie algebra structure of \(\mathrm{R}'\otimes_\mathrm{R}\mathfrak{g}\) being clear, let us prove its existence: when \(\mathfrak{g}\) is free with basis \((x_\alpha)\), by 5.3.1 there exists one and only one \(p\)-Lie algebra structure on the Lie algebra \(\mathrm{R}'\otimes_\mathrm{R}\mathfrak{g}\) such that \((1\otimes x_\alpha)^{(p)} = 1\otimes(x_\alpha^{(p)})\); this structure is the one we are looking for. When \(\mathfrak{g}\) is an arbitrary \(p\)-Lie algebra, there exist a \(p\)-Lie algebra \(\mathrm{L}_0\) that is free (as an R-module) and a surjective homomorphism \(q_0 : \mathrm{L}_0 \to \mathfrak{g}\); it suffices for example to take for \(\mathrm{L}_0\) the \(p\)-Lie algebra \(\mathrm{R}\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_p} \mathfrak{g}\), where \(\mathbb{F}_p\) denotes the prime field of characteristic \(p\), and for \(q_0\) the homomorphism \(\lambda\otimes x \mapsto \lambda\cdot x\) (\(\mathfrak{g}\) is free over \(\mathbb{F}_p\)!). The kernel of \(q_0\) is then a \(p\)-ideal of \(\mathrm{L}_0\), that is, an ideal of the Lie algebra \(\mathrm{L}_0\) that is stable under the endomorphism \(x \mapsto x^{(p)}\); there are therefore also a \(p\)-Lie algebra \(\mathrm{L}_1\) that is free (as an R-module) and a homomorphism \(q_1 : \mathrm{L}_1 \to \mathrm{L}_0\) whose image is \(\mathrm{Ker}\,q_0\): \[ \xymatrix{ L_1 \ar[r]^-{q_1} & L_0 \ar[r]^-{q_0} & \mathfrak{g} \ar[r] & 0 }. \] From this one deduces an exact sequence of R′-Lie algebras \[ \xymatrix{ R'\otimes_R L_1 \ar[r]^-{R'\otimes_R q_1} & R'\otimes_R L_0 \ar[r]^-{R'\otimes_R q_0} & R'\otimes_R \mathfrak{g} \ar[r] & 0 }. \] Since \(\mathrm{R}'\otimes_\mathrm{R} q_1\) is manifestly a homomorphism of \(p\)-Lie algebras, the kernel of \(\mathrm{R}'\otimes_\mathrm{R} q_0\) is a \(p\)-ideal, so that the symbolic \(p\)-th power operation of \(\mathrm{R}'\otimes_\mathrm{R} \mathrm{L}_0\) induces, by passage to the quotient, a map from \(\mathrm{R}'\otimes_\mathrm{R} \mathfrak{g}\) to \(\mathrm{R}'\otimes_\mathrm{R} \mathfrak{g}\) (use formula (ii) of 5.2); the latter equips \(\mathfrak{g}\) with the desired \(p\)-Lie algebra structure.

The canonical map \(j_\mathfrak{g}\) from \(\mathfrak{g}\) into the restricted enveloping algebra \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\) induces, for every extension \(f : \mathrm{R} \to \mathrm{R}'\) of the base ring, a homomorphism \[ \mathrm{R}'\otimes_\mathrm{R} j_\mathfrak{g} : \ \mathrm{R}'\otimes_\mathrm{R}\mathfrak{g} \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}'\otimes_\mathrm{R}\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g}), \] whence a homomorphism \(h\) from \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathrm{R}'\otimes_\mathrm{R}\mathfrak{g})\) to \(\mathrm{R}'\otimes_\mathrm{R}\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\) such that \(h \circ j_{R'\otimes\mathfrak{g}} = \mathrm{R}'\otimes_\mathrm{R} j_\mathfrak{g}\). It follows obviously from the universal properties of \(\mathrm{R}'\otimes_\mathrm{R}\mathfrak{g}\) and of the restricted enveloping algebra that \(h\) is an isomorphism, which will allow us to identify \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathrm{R}'\otimes_\mathrm{R}\mathfrak{g})\) with \(\mathrm{R}'\otimes_\mathrm{R}\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\).

In particular, if \(r\) is an element of R and if R′ is the localized ring \(\mathrm{R}_r\), one sees that \(\mathfrak{g}_r \simeq \mathrm{R}_r\otimes_\mathrm{R}\mathfrak{g}\) is canonically equipped with a \(p\)-Lie algebra structure over \(\mathrm{R}_r\), so that the sheaf \(\widetilde{\mathfrak{g}}\) on \(\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathrm{R}\) is a quasi-coherent \(p\)-Lie Algebra on \(\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathrm{R}\). Moreover, the restricted enveloping algebra \(\mathrm{U}_p^R(\mathfrak{g}_r)\) is identified with \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})_r\), so that the sheaf associated with the presheaf \(\mathrm{V} \mapsto \mathrm{U}_p(\Gamma(\mathrm{V},\mathfrak{g}))\) is quasi-coherent. More generally, if S is a scheme of characteristic \(p\) and \(\mathscr{G}\) a quasi-coherent \(p\)-Lie Algebra over \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\), the sheaf associated with the presheaf \(\mathrm{V} \mapsto \mathrm{U}_p(\Gamma(\mathrm{V},\mathscr{G}))\) is quasi-coherent; it will be denoted \(\mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{G})\) and called the restricted enveloping Algebra of \(\mathscr{G}\). If V is affine, \(\mathrm{U}_p(\Gamma(\mathrm{V},\mathscr{G}))\) is identified with the set of sections of \(\mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{G})\) over V.

The universal character of \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\) implies that \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\) is functorial in \(\mathfrak{g}\): every homomorphism of \(p\)-Lie algebras \(h : \mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{h}\) induces one and only one homomorphism of unital algebras \(\mathrm{U}_p(h)\) such that \(j_\mathfrak{h}\circ h = \mathrm{U}_p(h)\circ j_\mathfrak{g}\). Here are a few examples:

a) If \(\mathfrak{h} = 0\), \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{h})\) is identified with the base ring and \(\mathrm{U}_p(h)\) is a homomorphism of algebras \(\varepsilon_\mathfrak{g} : \mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g}) \to \mathrm{R}\) called the augmentation.

b) Let us now take for \(\mathfrak{h}\) the algebra \(\mathfrak{g}^\circ\) opposite to \(\mathfrak{g}\): \(\mathfrak{g}^\circ\) has the same underlying module as \(\mathfrak{g}\), the same symbolic \(p\)-th power, the bracket of two elements in \(\mathfrak{g}^\circ\) being the opposite of the bracket in \(\mathfrak{g}\). It is clear that we may identify \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g}^\circ)\) with the algebra opposite to \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\). Moreover, the isomorphism \(x \mapsto -x\) from \(\mathfrak{g}\) onto \(\mathfrak{g}^\circ\) induces an isomorphism \(c_\mathfrak{g}\) from \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\) onto \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g}^\circ) \simeq \mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})^\circ\). One says that \(c_\mathfrak{g}\) is the antipodism of \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\).

c) Finally, let \(\mathfrak{f}\) and \(\mathfrak{g}\) be two \(p\)-Lie algebras and \(\mathfrak{h}\) the product \(p\)-Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{f} \times \mathfrak{g}\), whose underlying R-module is the direct product \(\mathfrak{f} \times \mathfrak{g}\), the bracket and the symbolic power being defined by the formulas \[ \big[(x,y),(x',y')\big] = \big([x,x'],[y,y']\big) \qquad \text{and} \qquad (x,y)^{(p)} = (x^{(p)},y^{(p)}). \]

If \(h_1 : \mathfrak{f} \to \mathfrak{k}\) and \(h_2 : \mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{k}\) are two homomorphisms of \(p\)-Lie algebras such that \([h_1(x),h_2(y)] = 0\) for every \(x\) in \(\mathfrak{f}\) and every \(y\) in \(\mathfrak{g}\), the map \(h_1+h_2 : (x,y) \to h_1(x)+h_2(y)\) is a homomorphism of \(p\)-Lie algebras; conversely, every homomorphism from \(\mathfrak{f}\times\mathfrak{g}\) to \(\mathfrak{k}\) is of this type, which allows one to characterize \(\mathfrak{f}\times\mathfrak{g}\) as the solution of a universal problem. For example, the maps \(h_1 : x \mapsto i_\mathfrak{f}(x)\otimes 1\) and \(h_2 : y \mapsto 1\otimes i_\mathfrak{g}(y)\) induce a homomorphism \(h_1+h_2\) from \(\mathfrak{f} \times \mathfrak{g}\) into the \(p\)-Lie algebra underlying \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{f})\otimes \mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\). It follows from the universal characters of \(\mathfrak{f}\times\mathfrak{g}\) and of the restricted enveloping algebras that \(h_1+h_2\) extends to an isomorphism \(\varphi\) from \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{f}\times\mathfrak{g})\) onto the tensor product \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{f})\otimes \mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\).

If \(\mathfrak{f} = \mathfrak{g}\), the diagonal map \(\delta : x \mapsto (x,x)\) from \(\mathfrak{g}\) to \(\mathfrak{g}\times\mathfrak{g}\) induces a homomorphism from \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\) to \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g}\times\mathfrak{g})\). We shall denote by \(\Delta_\mathfrak{g}\) the composite of this homomorphism with \(\varphi\); one easily sees that \(\Delta_\mathfrak{g}\) and the multiplication of the algebra \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\) make \((\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g}),\Delta_\mathfrak{g})\) an R-coalgebra in groups (3.2) having \(\varepsilon_\mathfrak{g}\) as augmentation and \(c_\mathfrak{g}\) as antipodism.

Likewise, let S be a scheme of characteristic \(p\) and \(\mathscr{G}\) an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-\(p\)-Lie Algebra. As V runs through the open subsets of S, the coalgebra-in-groups structures defined previously on the sets \(\mathrm{U}_p(\Gamma(\mathrm{V},\mathscr{G}))\) induce on the restricted enveloping algebra \(\mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{G})\) a structure of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-\(p\)-Coalgebra in groups. By 5.3.1, the corresponding S-group functor \(\mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{G})\) associates with every S-scheme T the set of sections \(x\) of \(\mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{G}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_T)\) such that \[ \varepsilon(x) = 1 \qquad \text{and} \qquad \Delta x = x\otimes x. \]

Here \(\varepsilon\) denotes the augmentation of \(\mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{G}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_T)\), \(\Delta\) the diagonal morphism, and \(x\otimes x\) the canonical image of \((x,x)\) in \(\Gamma(\mathrm{T},\mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{G}_T)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_T}\mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{G}_T))\).

When \(\mathscr{G}\) is locally free of finite type as an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module, \(\mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{G})\) is representable by a finite, locally free S-scheme (5.3.3 and 3.1.2).

pages.title: 6. p-Lie algebra of an S-group scheme

blocks.html: 6. p-Lie algebra of an S-group scheme

Let S be a scheme of characteristic \(p > 0\). In paragraph 5 we associated with every quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-\(p\)-Lie Algebra \(\mathscr{G}\) an S-group functor \(\mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{G})\). We shall now see that, for every S-group scheme G, the Lie Algebra \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) defined in II 4.11 is naturally equipped with a structure of \(\mathbf{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-\(p\)-Lie algebra.

Let us first identify \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}\ \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\) respectively with Lie subalgebras of \(\mathrm{U}(\mathrm{G})\) and \(\mathrm{Dif}_{G/S}\) by means of the injections \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) of 2.5. The Lie Algebra of \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}\ \mathrm{G}\) is thus identified with the Lie algebra of S-derivations of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X}\). By 5.2, the latter is a \(p\)-Lie subalgebra of \(\mathrm{Dif}_{G/S}\).

On the other hand, by II 4.1.4, the image of \(\mathrm{L} = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\) under the right translation \(r : \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{G}) \to \mathrm{Dif}_{G/S}\) consists of the right invariant derivations. If \(x\) belongs to L, \(r(x)^p\) is none other than \(r(x^p)\) by 2.2. Since \(r(x)^p\) is again a derivation, one sees that \(x^p\) belongs to \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\), which is therefore a p-Lie subalgebra of the infinitesimal algebra U(G).

Let \(h : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\) be a homomorphism of S-group schemes. It is clear that the homomorphisms \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(h/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\) and \(\mathrm{U}(h)\) are compatible with the identifications of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{H}/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\) with \(p\)-Lie subalgebras of \(\mathrm{U}(\mathrm{G})\) and \(\mathrm{U}(\mathrm{H})\). Since \(\mathrm{U}(h)\) is a homomorphism of algebras, one thus sees that \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(h/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\) is a homomorphism of \(p\)-Lie algebras.

Likewise, if \(s : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{S}\) is a base change, the map from \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\) to \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{T})\) induced by \(s\) is a homomorphism of \(p\)-Lie algebras.

One can express this by saying that the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) is equipped with a structure of \(\mathbf{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-\(p\)-Lie algebra. In particular, as T runs through the open subsets of S, one sees that the sheaf \(\mathscr{L}ie(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) is equipped with a structure of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-\(p\)-Lie algebra.

Following an idea of Demazure, we shall now generalize the above to certain S-group functors that are not necessarily representable. To this end, we shall first give another definition of the symbolic \(p\)-th power in the Lie algebra of an S-group scheme G.

Let D be a derivation of G at the origin, that is, the deviation of the origin obtained by composing the canonical deviation \(\delta : \mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{I}_\mathrm{S}\) of 1.5 with an extension \(x\) to \(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{S}\) of the unit section \(\varepsilon : \mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{G}\). According to the definition we gave in 2.1, \(\mathrm{D}^p\) is the following composite deviation \[ \xymatrix{ S \simeq \underbrace{S\times S\times\cdots\times S}_{p} \ar[r]^-{\delta\times\cdots\times\delta} & I_S\times\cdots\times I_S \ar[r]^-{x\times\cdots\times x} & G\times\cdots\times G \ar[r]^-{m^{(p)}} & G } \] where \(m^{(p)}\) is the morphism induced by the multiplication \(m : \mathrm{G} \times \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}\). Since \(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{S}\times\cdots\times \mathrm{I}_\mathrm{S}\) is affine over S and has as affine Algebra \(\mathscr{O}_S[d_1,\ldots,d_p]/(d_1^2,\ldots,d_p^2)\), the deviation \(\delta\times\cdots\times\delta\) is defined by a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules \[ \mathscr{O}_S[d_1,\ldots,d_p]/(d_1^2,\ldots,d_p^2) \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_S \] that sends the monomial \(d_1d_2\cdots d_p\) to 1 and the other monomials \(d_{i_1}\cdots d_{i_r}\) to 0 \((r<p)\). On the other hand, if \(\mathrm{pr}_i\) denotes the projection of \(\mathrm{I}_S^p\) onto the \(i\)-th factor and if \(x_i\) is the image of \(x\) in \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{I}_S^p)\) under \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{pr}_i)\), the composite morphism \(m^{(p)} \circ (x\times\cdots\times x)\) is none other than the product \(x_1x_2\cdots x_p\). Consequently, \(\mathrm{D}^p\) is also the following composite deviation \[ \xymatrix{ S \ar[r]^-{\delta\times\cdots\times\delta} & I_S\times\cdots\times I_S \ar[r]^-{x_1x_2\cdots x_p} & G }. \]

This description allows us to prove again that \(\mathrm{D}^p\) is a derivation of G at the origin: indeed, since G is a very good group (II 4.11), the images \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{pr}_1)(x)\) and \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{pr}_2)(x)\) of \(x\) in \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{I}_S\times \mathrm{I}_S)\) commute with each other. It follows that the elements \(x_i\) of \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{I}_S^p)\) commute pairwise, in other words that, for every permutation \(\sigma\) of the factors of \(\mathrm{I}_S^p\), we have \((x_1\cdots x_p)\circ\sigma = x_1\cdots x_p\); it follows that \(x_1\cdots x_p\) factors through the canonical projection of \(\mathrm{I}_S^p\) into the symmetric product \(\Sigma^p \mathrm{I}_S\) (4.2).

The symmetric product \(\Sigma^p \mathrm{I}_S\) has as affine Algebra a sub-Algebra A of \(\mathscr{O}_S[d_1,\ldots,d_p]/(d_1^2,\ldots,d_p^2)\) whose basis over \(\mathscr{O}_S\) consists of the elementary symmetric functions \(1=\sigma_0,\sigma_1,\ldots,\sigma_p\) of \(d_1,\ldots,d_p\). We denote by \(q\) the homomorphism from A to \(\mathscr{O}_S[d]/(d^2)\) that annihilates \(\sigma_1,\ldots,\sigma_{p-1}\) and sends \(\sigma_p\) to \(d\), and by \(i\) the closed immersion of \(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{S}\) into \(\Sigma^p \mathrm{I}_S\) associated with \(q\). We then have a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ S \ar[d]_-{\delta} \ar[r]^-{\delta\times\cdots\times\delta} \ar@/^2pc/[rr]^-{D^p} & I_S^p \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{can.}} \ar[r]^-{x_1\cdots x_p} & G \ar@{=}[d] \\ I_S \ar[r]_-{i} & \Sigma^p I_S \ar[r]_-{y} & G } \] which shows that \(\mathrm{D}^p\) is of the form \(y \circ \delta\), q.e.d.

Let \(\mathfrak{S}_p\) be the symmetric group of order \(p\) and \(\mathrm{I}_S^p \times \mathfrak{S}_p\) the direct sum of a family of copies of \(\mathrm{I}_S^p\) indexed by \(\mathfrak{S}_p\). We denote by \(\pi : \mathrm{I}_S^p\times\mathfrak{S}_p \to \mathrm{I}_S^p\) the canonical projection and by \(k : \mathrm{I}_S^p\times\mathfrak{S}_p \to \mathrm{I}_S^p\) the morphism defining the action of \(\mathfrak{S}_p\) on \(\mathrm{I}_S^p\) (if \(\sigma\) is an element of \(\mathfrak{S}_p\), the restriction of \(k\) to \(\mathrm{I}_S^p\times\sigma\) has \(\mathrm{pr}_{\sigma j}\) as \(j\)-th component). This being so, we say that a functor \(\mathrm{X} : (\mathbf{Sch}/\mathrm{S})^\circ \to (\mathbf{Ens})\) satisfies condition (F) if X transforms finite direct sums into direct products and if, for every S-scheme T, the sequence \[ \xymatrix{ X(T\times_S \Sigma^p I_S) \ar[r] & X(T\times_S I_S^p) \ar@<0.7ex>[r]^-{X(T\times_S \pi)} \ar@<-0.7ex>[r]_-{X(T\times_S k)} & X(T\times_S I_S^p\times \mathfrak{S}_p) } \] is exact. Every S-scheme satisfies (F); if \(\mathscr{F}\) is an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module, \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\) satisfies (F); every projective limit of functors satisfying (F) also satisfies (F); if \(\underline{\mathrm{Y}}\) satisfies (F) and X is an arbitrary S-functor, \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(\mathrm{X},\mathrm{Y})\) satisfies (F).

Let X be a very good group (II 4.10) satisfying condition (F). Denoting by \(x : \mathrm{I}_S \to \mathrm{X}\) a morphism extending the unit section of X and resuming the notation of 6.2, one sees as above that \(x_1\cdots x_p : \mathrm{I}_S^p \to \mathrm{X}\) factors through \(\Sigma^p \mathrm{I}_S\): \[ \xymatrix{ I_S^p \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{can.}} \ar[rr]^-{x_1\cdots x_p} & & X \\ \Sigma^p I_S \ar[urr]_-{\tau} & & } \] and defines by composition a morphism \[ x^{(p)} : \ I_S \xrightarrow{\ i\ } \Sigma^p I_S \xrightarrow{\ \tau\ } X \] which we shall call the symbolic \(p\)-th power of \(x\). The endomorphism \(x \mapsto x^{(p)}\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\) is obviously compatible with base change and is functorial in G. It would be interesting to know for which G this endomorphism makes \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\) a \(p\)-Lie algebra.
The last definition of the symbolic \(p\)-th power, which we have just given, is particularly well suited to computation. Here are a few examples:

Let M be an "abstract" abelian group and \(\mathbf{D}_S(\mathrm{M})\) the diagonalizable S-group of type M (I 4.4.2). For every S-scheme T, we thus have \(\mathbf{D}_S(\mathrm{M})(\mathrm{T}) = \mathrm{Hom}_{(Ab)}(\mathrm{M}, \mathbf{O}(\mathrm{T})^*)\). Let \(x\) be an element of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathbf{D}_S(\mathrm{M})/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\), that is, a homomorphism of abelian groups \[ \xymatrix{ M \ar[r]^-{x} & \Gamma(S,\mathscr{O}_S+d\mathscr{O}_S)^* } \] of the form \(m \mapsto 1+d\,\xi(m)\), where \(\xi \in \mathrm{Hom}_{(Ab)}(\mathrm{M},\mathbf{O}(\mathrm{S}))\). With the notation of 6.2 and 6.3, the product \(x_1\cdots x_p\) associates with an element \(m\) of M the expression \[ (1+d_1\xi(m))\cdots(1+d_p\xi(m)) \] that is, \(1+\sigma_1\xi(m)+\sigma_2(\xi(m))^2+\cdots+\sigma_p(\xi(m))^p\).

This expression does indeed belong to \(\mathbf{O}(\Sigma^p \mathrm{I}_S)\). Projecting the affine Algebra A of \(\Sigma^p \mathrm{I}_S\) into \(\mathscr{O}_S[d]/(d^2)\) by annihilating \(\sigma_1,\ldots,\sigma_{p-1}\) and sending \(\sigma_p\) to \(d\), one sees that \(x^{(p)}\) is the homomorphism \[ m \mapsto 1+d(\xi(m))^p \] from M to \(\Gamma(\mathrm{S},\mathscr{O}_S+d\mathscr{O}_S)\).

In summary, if one identifies \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathbf{D}_S(\mathrm{M})/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\) with \(\mathrm{Hom}_{(Ab)}(\mathrm{M},\mathbf{O}(\mathrm{S})^*)\) as in 5.1, the symbolic \(p\)-th power associates with \(\xi\) the homomorphism \(\xi^{(p)} : m \mapsto \xi(m)^p\).

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module and G the S-functor in abelian groups \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\) (cf. I 4.6). Let \(y\) be an element of \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})(\mathrm{S}) = \Gamma(\mathrm{S},\mathscr{F})\) and \(y'\) the canonical image of \(y\) in \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})(\mathrm{I}_S)\). One knows that the map \(y \mapsto d\,y'\) is a bijection from \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})(\mathrm{S})\) onto \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\). If one sets \(x = d\,y'\), the quantity \(x_i\) of 6.2 is none other than \(d_i\,y''\), where \(y''\) denotes the canonical image of \(x\) in \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})(\mathrm{I}_S^p)\). Consequently the product \(x_1\cdots x_p\) equals \((d_1+\cdots+d_p)y''\) and belongs to \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})(\Sigma^p \mathrm{I}_S)\). Since the map from \(\mathbf{O}(\Sigma^p \mathrm{I}_S)\) to \(\mathbf{O}(\mathrm{I}_S)\) defining the morphism \(i\) of 6.1 annihilates \(d_1+\cdots+d_p\), one sees that \(x^{(p)}\) is zero.
For every \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module \(\mathscr{F}\), the symbolic p-th power operation in the Lie algebra of \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\) is therefore zero.

Let X be an S-scheme, G the S-group functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_S \mathrm{X}\) and D an S-derivation of the structure sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X}\). By 6.1, D may be identified with an \(\mathrm{I}_S\)-automorphism \(x\) of \(\mathrm{X}_{I_S}\) which may be described as follows: if \(s\) is a section of \(\mathscr{O}_S[d]/(d^2)\) of the form \(a+db\), let us set \(\mathrm{D}_{I_S} s = \mathrm{D}a + d(\mathrm{D}b)\); in other words, \(\mathrm{D}_{I_S}\) is deduced from D by the base change \(\mathrm{I}_S \to \mathrm{S}\); the automorphism in question of \(\mathrm{X}_{I_S}\) is then associated with the endomorphism \(s \mapsto s+d(\mathrm{D}_{I_S} s)\) of \(\mathscr{O}_S[d]/(d^2)\).

Likewise, let \(\mathrm{D}_{I_S^p}\) be the differential operator of \(\mathrm{X}_{I_S^p}\) deduced from D by the base change \(\mathrm{I}_S^p \to \mathrm{S}\). With the notation of 6.2, the automorphism \(x_i\) of \(\mathrm{X}_{I_S^p}\) is then associated with the endomorphism \(s \mapsto s+d_i(\mathrm{D}_{I_S^p}(s))\) of \(\mathscr{O}_S[d_1,\ldots,d_p]/(d_1^2,\ldots,d_p^2)\). The product \(x_1\cdots x_p\) is therefore associated with the endomorphism \[ (1+d_1\mathrm{D}_{I_S^p})(1+d_2\mathrm{D}_{I_S^p})\cdots(1+d_p\mathrm{D}_{I_S^p}) \] that is, with \(1+\sigma_1\mathrm{D}_{I_S^p}+\sigma_2(\mathrm{D}_{I_S^p})^2+\cdots+\sigma_p(\mathrm{D}_{I_S^p})^p\).

The coefficient of \(\sigma_p\) is \((\mathrm{D}_{I_S^p})^p\), which means that the bijection \(\mathrm{D} \mapsto x\) from the Lie algebra of S-derivations of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X}\) onto the Lie algebra of \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_S \mathrm{X}\) is an isomorphism of \(p\)-Lie algebras.

Using the same method, one sees that, for every \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module \(\mathscr{F}\), the bijection described in II 4.5 is an isomorphism of \(p\)-Lie algebras from \(\mathrm{End}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}} \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\) onto \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}} \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\).

Likewise, if \(\mathscr{U}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Coalgebra in groups, and if G is the group functor \(\mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U}\), one easily sees that the canonical injection of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\) into \(\Gamma(\mathrm{S},\mathscr{U})\), which identifies \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\) with the set of primitive elements of \(\Gamma(\mathrm{S},\mathscr{U})\), is compatible with the \(p\)-th power.
7. Radicial groups of height 1

Let S be a scheme of characteristic \(p>0\). We shall say that an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Algebra \(\mathscr{A}\) (resp. an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-\(p\)-Lie algebra \(\mathscr{L}\)) is finite locally free if the \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module underlying \(\mathscr{A}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{L}\)) is locally free and of finite type. If \(\mathscr{L}\) is a finite locally free \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-\(p\)-Lie algebra, we know that the S-group functor \[ \mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L}) = \mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{L}) \] is representable by a finite, locally free S-scheme (5.4.1). We shall see that this S-scheme is the solution of a universal problem and we shall characterize the S-group schemes of the form \(\mathrm{Spec}^*\,\mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{L})\).

Let us first consider a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-\(p\)-Lie Algebra \(\mathscr{L}\). As V runs through the open subsets of S, the maps \(j : \Gamma(\mathrm{V},\mathscr{L}) \to \mathrm{U}_p(\Gamma(\mathrm{V},\mathscr{L}))\) of 5.3 define a morphism \(\underline{j} : \mathscr{L} \to \mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{L})\). On the other hand, by 3.2.3, the Lie \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Algebra of the S-group functor \(\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L})\) is the kernel of the morphism \[ \Delta - \mathrm{in}_1 - \mathrm{in}_2 : \ \mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{L}) \longrightarrow \mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{L}) \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{L}), \] where \(\Delta\) denotes the diagonal morphism and \(\mathrm{in}_1\), \(\mathrm{in}_2\) are the injections \(x \mapsto x\otimes 1\) and \(x \mapsto 1\otimes x\). It is clear that the image of \(\underline{j}\) is contained in the kernel \(\mathscr{L}ie\,\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L})\) of \(\Delta - \mathrm{in}_1 - \mathrm{in}_2\); this is why we shall denote by \(\underline{j}_\mathscr{L} : \mathscr{L} \to \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}\,\mathcal{G}_p(\mathscr{L})\) the morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-\(p\)-Lie Algebras induced by \(\underline{j}\) (6.4.4).

Now consider a very good S-group functor G satisfying condition (F) of 6.3 and let \(h : \mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L}) \to \mathrm{G}\) be a homomorphism of S-group functors. By 6.3, the morphism \(\mathscr{L}ie\,h : \mathscr{L}ie\,\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L}) \to \mathscr{L}ie\,\mathrm{G}\) is a homomorphism of Lie \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Algebras that is compatible with raising to the symbolic \(p\)-th power. The same therefore holds for the composite morphism \((\mathscr{L}ie\,h)\circ \underline{j}_\mathscr{L}\). If we denote by \(\mathrm{Hom}_p(\mathscr{L},\mathscr{L}ie\,\mathrm{G})\) the set of homomorphisms of Lie \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Algebras that are compatible with raising to the symbolic \(p\)-th power, we thus have a map \(\mathrm{J}(\mathscr{L},\mathrm{G}) : h \mapsto (\mathscr{L}ie\,h)\circ \underline{j}_\mathscr{L}\) from \(\mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-Gr.}}(\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L}), \mathrm{G})\) to \(\mathrm{Hom}_p(\mathscr{L}, \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}\,\mathrm{G})\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 7.2

If \(\mathscr{L}\) is a finite locally free \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-\(p\)-Lie Algebra, the map \[ \mathrm{J}(\mathscr{L},\mathrm{G}) : \ \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L}), \mathrm{G}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_p(\mathscr{L}, \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}\,\mathrm{G}) \] is bijective in each of the following cases:

(i) G is an S-group scheme;

(ii) G is of the form \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_S\ \mathrm{X}\), where X is an S-scheme;

(iii) G is of one of the forms \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\) or \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod}}\ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\), where \(\mathscr{F}\) denotes a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module.
The proof of the theorem relies on the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma

If \(\mathscr{L}\) is a finite locally free \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-\(p\)-Lie Algebra, the S-group \(\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L})\) is annihilated by the Frobenius morphism of \(\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L})\) relative to S.

Indeed, let \(\mathscr{U}\) be the restricted enveloping Algebra of \(\mathscr{L}\) and let us set \(\mathscr{A} = \mathscr{U}^* = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{U},\mathscr{O}_S)\). Then \(\mathscr{A}\) is the affine Algebra of \(\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L})\). Moreover, if \(\mathscr{J}\) is the augmentation ideal of \(\mathscr{U}\), that is, the Ideal generated by the image of \(\underline{j}_\mathscr{L} : \mathscr{L} \to \mathscr{U}\), we denote by \(\mathscr{I}\) the orthogonal of \(\mathscr{J}\) in \(\mathscr{A}\). We thus have \(\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{I} \simeq \mathscr{O}_S\) and the Ideal \(\mathscr{I}\) defines the unit section of \(\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L})\).

If \(\pi\) is the endomorphism \(x \mapsto x^p\) of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\), we must show that the morphism \(\Phi : a\otimes x \mapsto ax^p\) from \(\mathscr{O}_S \otimes_\pi \mathscr{A}\) to \(\mathscr{A}\) vanishes on \(\mathscr{O}_S \otimes_\pi \mathscr{I}\). Now \(\Phi\) is none other than the following composite \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{O}_S\otimes_\pi \mathscr{A} \ar[r]^-{j(\mathscr{A})} & \mathscr{S}^p\mathscr{A} \ar[r]^-{b(\mathscr{A})} & \mathscr{A} } \] where \(b(\mathscr{A})\) and \(j(\mathscr{A})\) are defined as in 4.3.3. Since the \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module dual to \(\mathscr{S}^p\mathscr{A}\) is none other than the sub-Module \(\Sigma^p\mathscr{U}\) of \(\bigotimes^p\mathscr{U}\) consisting of the sections invariant under the action of the symmetric group of order \(p\), one sees that the transpose \(\Phi^*\) of \(\Phi\) is the following composite morphism: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{U} \ar[r]^-{a(\mathscr{U})} & \Sigma^p\mathscr{U} \ar[r]^-{r(\mathscr{U})} & \mathscr{U}\otimes_\pi \mathscr{O}_S } \] where \(a(\mathscr{U})\) is induced by the morphism \((\Delta\otimes\mathscr{U}\otimes\cdots\otimes\mathscr{U})\cdots(\Delta\otimes\mathscr{U})\Delta\) from \(\mathscr{U}\) to \(\bigotimes^p\mathscr{U}\) (\(\Delta\) is the diagonal morphism of \(\mathscr{U}\)); likewise, \(r(\mathscr{U})\) vanishes on the symmetrized tensors and sends a section \(x\otimes\cdots\otimes x\) to \(x\otimes_\pi 1\) (see 4.3.3).

It now remains to show that \(\Phi^*\) annihilates the augmentation ideal \(\mathscr{J}\). Since \(\Phi^*\) is a homomorphism of Algebras, it suffices to see that \(\Phi^*\) vanishes on \(\mathrm{Im}\,\underline{j}_\mathscr{L}\). This follows from the formula \(\Delta s = s\otimes 1 + 1\otimes s\) for \(s \in \mathrm{Im}\,\underline{j}_\mathscr{L}\).

Let us set \(\mathrm{G}_p = \mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L})\). We shall first prove assertion (ii) of Theorem 7.2, keeping the notation above. Since every element of \(\mathscr{I}\) has zero \(p\)-th power and \(\mathscr{I}\) is an \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module of finite type, \(\mathscr{I}\) is locally nilpotent. We thus have \((\mathrm{G}_p)_{\mathrm{réd}} = \mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{réd}}\). Now the homomorphisms \(h\) from \(\mathrm{G}_p\) to \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}\ \mathrm{X}\) correspond one-to-one to the left actions \(h' : \mathrm{G}_p \times \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{X}\) of \(\mathrm{G}_p\) on X. For such an action, if \(\varepsilon\) is the unit section of \(\mathrm{G}_p\), the composite morphism \[ \xymatrix{ X \simeq S\times X \ar[r]^-{\varepsilon\times X} & G_p\times X \ar[r]^-{h'} & X } \] must be the identity. Since \((\mathrm{G}_p\times \mathrm{X})_{\mathrm{réd}}\) is identified with \(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{réd}}\), one sees that \(h'\) must induce the identity on the associated reduced schemes. In particular, \(h'\) induces an action of \(\mathrm{G}_p\) on all the open subsets of X, so that one easily reduces to the case where S and X are affine, or more generally to the case where X is affine over S. In this latter case, one applies the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma

Let X be an affine S-scheme with Algebra \(\mathscr{C}\) and \(\mathrm{G}_p\) a finite locally free S-group scheme with Algebra \(\mathscr{A}\). If we set \(\mathscr{U} = \mathscr{A}^* = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{A},\mathscr{O}_S)\), the left actions of \(\mathrm{G}_p\) on X correspond one-to-one to the representations of the algebra \(\mathscr{U}\) in the \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module \(\mathscr{C}\) such that \[ u(1_\mathscr{C}) = \varepsilon(u)\cdot 1_\mathscr{C} \qquad \text{and} \qquad u(xy) = \sum_i v_i(x)w_i(y) \quad \text{if} \quad \Delta u = \sum_i v_i\otimes w_i. \]

In these formulas, \(u\) denotes an arbitrary section of \(\mathscr{U}\) over an affine open subset V, and \(x\) and \(y\) sections of \(\mathscr{C}\) over V; we denote by \(1_\mathscr{C}\) the unit section of \(\mathscr{C}\), and by \(\varepsilon\) and \(\Delta\) the augmentation and the diagonal morphism of \(\mathscr{U}\). A left action \(h'\) of G on X is defined by a homomorphism of Algebras \(\lambda : \mathscr{C} \to \mathscr{A} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{C}\). We shall denote by \(\mu\) the composite morphism \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{U}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{C} \ar[r]^-{U\otimes\lambda} & \mathscr{U}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{A}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{C} \ar[r]^-{\gamma\otimes\mathscr{C}} & \mathscr{O}_S\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{C} \simeq \mathscr{C} } \] where \(\gamma\) is the "contraction" of \(\mathscr{A}^*\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{A}\) into \(\mathscr{O}_S\). One knows that the map \(\lambda \mapsto (\gamma\otimes\mathscr{C})(\mathscr{U}\otimes\lambda)\) is a bijection from \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{C},\mathscr{A}\otimes\mathscr{C})\) onto \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{U}\otimes\mathscr{C},\mathscr{C})\). Moreover, one easily sees that \(\lambda\) is a homomorphism of unital Algebras defining an action of \(\mathrm{G}_p\) on X if and only if \(\mu\) satisfies the conditions of the lemma.

It is moreover clear that, for every representation of \(\mathscr{U}\) in the \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module \(\mathscr{C}\), the sections \(u\) of \(\mathscr{U}\) satisfying the conditions of the preceding lemma form a sub-Algebra of \(\mathscr{U}\). In the particular case of interest to us, these conditions are therefore satisfied for all sections \(u\) if they hold for the sections \(u\) of \(\mathrm{Im}\,\underline{j}_\mathscr{L}\). If \(u\) is a section of \(\mathrm{Im}\,\underline{j}_\mathscr{L}\), these conditions mean that \(u(1_\mathscr{C}) = 0\) and that \(u(xy) = u(x)y+xu(y)\). Every homomorphism \(h\) from \(\mathrm{G}_p = \mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L})\) to \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}\ \mathrm{X}\) therefore defines a homomorphism H from \(\mathscr{U}\) to \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{C},\mathscr{C})\) sending \(\mathrm{Im}\,\underline{j}_\mathscr{L}\) into the set of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-derivations of \(\mathscr{C}\). The map \(\mathrm{H}\circ \underline{j}_\mathscr{L}\) is a homomorphism of \(p\)-Lie Algebras from \(\mathscr{L}\) into the sheaf \(\mathrm{D\acute{e}r}_{X/S}\) of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-derivations of \(\mathscr{C}\). Moreover, the map \(h \mapsto \mathrm{H}\circ \underline{j}_\mathscr{L}\) is obviously bijective; it would remain to check that, upon identifying \(\mathrm{D\acute{e}r}_{X/S}\) with \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{Aut}\ \mathrm{X}/\mathrm{S})\) as in 2.5, one identifies the map \(h \mapsto \mathrm{H}\circ \underline{j}_\mathscr{L}\) with that of Theorem 7.2.

Let us now show how assertion (i) of Theorem 7.2 follows from (ii): if T is an S-scheme and \(x\) an element of \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T})\), we denote by \(\ell_x^T\) (resp. \(r_x^T\)) the left (resp. right) translation of \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{T}\) defined by \(x\). The maps \(\ell^T : x \mapsto \ell_x^T\) thus determine a homomorphism \(\ell\) from G to \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}\ \mathrm{G}\). On the other hand, let \(f\) be a T-automorphism of \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{T}\); one then defines \({}^xf\) as being equal to \((r_x^T)^{-1}f r_x^T\); in this way G acts on the left on the S-functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}\ \mathrm{G}\), hence also on the functors \(\mathrm{T} \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{T\text{-Gr.}}(\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L}_T), \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}\ \mathrm{X}_T)\) and \(\mathrm{T} \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_p(\mathscr{L}_T, \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}\ \mathrm{X}_T/\mathrm{T}))\). On the other hand, the homomorphism \(\ell\) induces commutative squares \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Hom}_{T\text{-Gr.}}(G_p(\mathscr{L}_T), G_T) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathrm{Hom}_p(\mathscr{L}_T, \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G_T/T)) \ar[d] \\ \mathrm{Hom}_{T\text{-Gr.}}(G_p(\mathscr{L}_T), \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}\ G_T) \ar[r] & \mathrm{Hom}_p(\mathscr{L}_T, \mathscr{L}ie(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}\ G_T/T)) } \] The images of the two vertical arrows are the subfunctors consisting of the invariants under the action of the S-group G. Since the second horizontal arrow is invertible by 7.2.1 and is compatible with the action of G, the first horizontal arrow is also invertible.

Finally, consider the case of \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}\ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\) (the case of \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\) is analogous). Let us set \(\mathrm{G}_p = \mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L})\). A homomorphism from \(\mathrm{G}_p\) to \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}\ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\) is a multiplicative homomorphism from \(\mathrm{G}_p\) to \(\underline{\mathrm{End}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}\ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\) that is compatible with the unit sections of \(\mathrm{G}_p\) and of \(\underline{\mathrm{End}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}\ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\). Now a morphism of S-functors \(h : \mathrm{G}_p \to \underline{\mathrm{End}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}\ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\) is by definition an endomorphism of \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{G}_p}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{F})\); by I 4.6.2 (ii), such an endomorphism is induced by an endomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{G}_p}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{F}\), that is, an \(\mathscr{A}\)-linear endomorphism of the sheaf \(\mathscr{A}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{F}\), where \(\mathscr{A}\) is the affine Algebra of \(\mathrm{G}_p\). Such an endomorphism is of the form \(a\otimes x \mapsto a\lambda(x)\), where \(\lambda\) is a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules from \(\mathscr{F}\) to \(\mathscr{A}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{F}\). If one sets \(\mu = (\gamma\otimes\mathscr{F})(\mathscr{U}\otimes\lambda)\) as in 7.2.1, \(h\) is finally determined by \(\mu : \mathscr{U}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{F}\). The hypotheses made on \(h\) translate into saying that \(\mu\) defines a Module structure on \(\mathscr{F}\). Such a Module structure is defined by a homomorphism of \(p\)-Lie Algebras from \(\mathscr{L}\) to \(\underline{\mathrm{End}}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{F})\), which equals \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}\ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})/\mathrm{S})\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.3

If \(\mathscr{L}\) is a finite locally free \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-\(p\)-Lie algebra, the morphism \(\underline{j}_\mathscr{L} : \mathscr{L} \to \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}\,\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L})\) of 7.1 is invertible.

Indeed, the problem is local on S. We may therefore assume that S is affine with ring R and that \(\mathscr{L}\) is the sheaf associated with an R-\(p\)-Lie algebra with basis \(x_1,\ldots,x_r\). We may then use the notation of 5.3.3 and set \(z^n = \prod_i z_i^{n_i}\) for every r-tuple \((n_1,\ldots,n_r)\) of natural integers such that \(0 \leqslant n_i < p\). Setting moreover \(n! = \prod_i (n_i)!\) and equipping the monoid \(\mathbb{N}^r\) with the product order, one easily sees that \[ \Delta z^n = \sum \frac{z^m}{m!}\otimes\frac{z^{n-m}}{(n-m)!} \] the sum being extended over all \(m\) in \(\mathbb{N}^r\) such that \(0\leqslant m\leqslant n\) (\(\Delta\) is the diagonal morphism of \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\)). Since the \(z^n\) form a basis of \(\mathrm{U}_p(\mathfrak{g})\), it is clear that \(\Delta x = x\otimes 1+1\otimes x\) if and only if \(x\) is a linear combination of \(z_1,\ldots,z_r\), q.e.d.

To conclude the exposé, we shall give a characterization of the S-group schemes of the form \(\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L})\), where \(\mathscr{L}\) is a finite locally free \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-\(p\)-Lie Algebra.

Let G be an S-group scheme, \(\varepsilon_\mathrm{G}\) the unit section and \(\mathrm{I}\) the kernel of the morphism from \(\varepsilon_\mathrm{G}^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G})\) to \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\) that defines \(\varepsilon_\mathrm{G}\). The canonical image of \(\mathrm{Lie}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\) in \(\mathrm{U}(\mathrm{G})\) (2.5) is identified, by 1.3, with the morphisms of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules from \(\varepsilon_\mathrm{G}^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G})\) to \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\) that vanish on the unit section of \(\varepsilon_\mathrm{G}^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G})\) and on \(\mathrm{I}^2\). One thus recovers the canonical isomorphism from \(\mathrm{Lie}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})(\mathrm{S})\) onto \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathrm{I}/\mathrm{I}^2, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S})\) of Exposé II. We shall moreover set \(\omega_{\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}} = \mathrm{I}/\mathrm{I}^2\) as in Exposé II, so that the sheaf \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) is identified with \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\omega_{\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S})\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem

If G is a group scheme over a scheme S of characteristic \(p>0\), the following assertions are equivalent:

(i) There exists a finite locally free \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-\(p\)-Lie Algebra \(\mathscr{L}\) such that G is isomorphic to \(\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L})\).

(ii) G is affine over S; \(\omega_{\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}}\) is a locally free \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module of finite type and the affine Algebra of G is locally isomorphic to the quotient of the symmetric Algebra \(\mathscr{S}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\omega_{\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}})\) by the Ideal generated by the \(p\)-th powers of the sections of \(\omega_{\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}}\).

(iii) G is locally of finite presentation over S, of height \(\leqslant 1\) (4.1.3) and \(\omega_{\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}}\) is locally free.(*)

(*) The condition on \(\omega_{\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}}\) is in fact unnecessary, as one easily sees by reducing to the case where S is local with residue field \(k\), and applying the theorem to the case of the Group \(\mathrm{G}_k\).

The implication (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (iii) being clear, let us first show that (i) implies (ii): to this end we consider the exact sequence \[ (*) \qquad 0 \longrightarrow \mathscr{L} \xrightarrow{\ \underline{j}_\mathscr{L}\ } \mathscr{J} \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } \mathscr{J}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{J}, \] where \(\mathscr{J}\) is the augmentation Ideal of \(\mathscr{U} = \mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{L})\) and \(\delta\) is the morphism induced by \(\Delta - \mathrm{in}_1 - \mathrm{in}_2\). If \(q\) is the projection from \(\mathscr{U}\) onto \(\mathscr{J}\) that vanishes on the unit section of \(\mathscr{U}\), \(\delta\) may also be characterized by the commutative square \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{U} \ar[r]^-{\Delta} \ar[d]_-{q} & \mathscr{U}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{U} \ar[d]^-{q\otimes q} \\ \mathscr{J} \ar[r]_-{\delta} & \mathscr{J}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{J}. } \]

Moreover, the sequence (*) remains exact after any base change; consequently (Bourb., Alg. Comm. II §3, prop. 6), the sequence (*) splits and gives by duality an exact sequence \[ \mathscr{I}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{I} \xrightarrow{\ m\ } \mathscr{I} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{L},\mathscr{O}_S) \longrightarrow 0, \] where \(\mathscr{I}\) still denotes the augmentation Ideal of the affine Algebra \(\mathscr{A}\) of \(\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L})\) and \(m\) is induced by the multiplication of \(\mathscr{A}\). This shows that \(\omega_{\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}}\) is the dual of \(\mathscr{L}\), hence is finite locally free.

Suppose now that S is affine. There is then a section \(\sigma : \omega_{\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}} \to \mathscr{I}\) of the canonical projection of \(\mathscr{I}\) onto \(\mathscr{I}/\mathscr{I}^2\); such a section induces (Lemma 7.2) a homomorphism of algebras \(h : \mathscr{S}_{\mathscr{O}_S}[\omega_{\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}}]/(x^p)_{x\in\omega_{\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}}} \to \mathscr{A}\). If one filters \(\mathscr{A}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{S}_{\mathscr{O}_S}[\omega]/(x^p)_{x\in\omega}\)) by the powers of \(\mathscr{I}\) (resp. of the Ideal generated by \(\omega_{\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}}\)), it is clear that \(h\) induces an epimorphism of the associated graded objects. Hence \(h\) is an epimorphism of locally free \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules of the same rank; hence \(h\) is an isomorphism.

Let us finally show that (iii) implies (i): since the Frobenius morphism annihilates G, it is clear that the unit section of G induces a homeomorphism from the topological space underlying S onto the space underlying G. We may therefore identify S with the closed subscheme of G defined by a certain Ideal \(\mathscr{I}\) of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}\). Since G is locally of finite presentation over S and every section of \(\mathscr{I}\) has zero \(p\)-th power, \(\mathscr{I}\) is locally nilpotent and G is affine over S (EGA I, 5.1.9), hence finite over S.

So let \(\mathscr{A}\) be the affine \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Algebra of G; let us set \(\mathscr{L} = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\), \(\mathscr{A}_p = \mathscr{U}_p(\mathscr{L})^*\) and let \(\mathrm{G}_p = \mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L})\) be the spectrum of \(\mathscr{A}_p\). By Theorem 7.2, the identity of \(\mathscr{L}\) is defined by a homomorphism of groups \(h : \mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L}) \to \mathrm{G}\), hence by a homomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Algebras \(a : \mathscr{A} \to \mathscr{A}_p\). It is a matter of showing that \(a\), which by definition induces a homomorphism from \(\omega_{\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}}\) onto \(\omega_{\mathrm{G}_p/\mathrm{S}}\), is an isomorphism:

To this end, we may restrict to the case where S is affine. There is then a section \(\tau\) of the canonical projection of \(\mathscr{I}\) onto \(\omega_{\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}}\). Since every section of \(\mathscr{I}\) has zero \(p\)-th power, \(\tau\) induces a homomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Algebras \[ b : \mathscr{S}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\omega_{\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S}})/\mathscr{K} \longrightarrow \mathscr{A}. \] It is clear that \(b\) is an epimorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules (see 7.4.1). On the other hand, we saw in 7.4.1 that \(ab\) is an isomorphism. The same therefore holds for \(a\), q.e.d.

pages.title: 8. The case of a base field

blocks.html: 8. The case of a base field

Let us now summarize the results obtained in the case where S is the spectrum of a field \(k\) of characteristic \(p>0\). Let us then say that an S-group scheme is algebraic if the underlying scheme is of finite type over S: by Theorem 7.2 the functor \(\mathrm{G}_p\), which associates with every \(k\)-\(p\)-Lie algebra of finite dimension over \(k\) the \(k\)-group \(\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L})\), is then left adjoint to the functor associating with every algebraic \(k\)-group its \(p\)-Lie algebra over \(k\). By 7.3 and Theorem 7.4.1, the functor \(\mathrm{G}_p : \mathscr{L} \mapsto \mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L})\) induces an equivalence from the category of \(k\)-\(p\)-Lie algebras of finite dimension onto that of algebraic \(k\)-groups of height \(\leqslant 1\). Since \(\mathrm{G}_p\) is a left adjoint functor, it commutes with inductive limits; since the inclusion of the category of algebraic \(k\)-groups of height \(\leqslant 1\) into that of all algebraic groups manifestly commutes with finite projective limits, one finally sees that \(\mathrm{G}_p\) is an exact functor: for example, if \(i : \mathscr{L}_0 \to \mathscr{L}_1\) and \(q : \mathscr{L}_1 \to \mathscr{L}_2\) are homomorphisms of \(k\)-\(p\)-Lie algebras and if the sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathscr{L}_0 \xrightarrow{\ i\ } \mathscr{L}_1 \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathscr{L}_2 \longrightarrow 0, \] formed by the underlying vector spaces, is exact, then \(\mathrm{G}_p(i)\) is an isomorphism from \(\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L}_0)\) onto the kernel of \(\mathrm{G}_p(q)\); the image of \(\mathrm{G}_p(i)\) is therefore a normal subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L}_1)\) and \(\mathrm{G}_p(q)\) induces an isomorphism from the quotient of \(\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L}_1)\) by this normal subgroup onto \(\mathrm{G}_p(\mathscr{L}_2)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.2

Consider an exact sequence of algebraic groups over a field \(k\) of characteristic \(p>0\) \[ 1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}' \xrightarrow{\ v\ } \mathrm{G} \xrightarrow{\ u\ } \mathrm{G}'' \longrightarrow 1 \] and the following assertions:

(i) The morphism \(u\) is smooth.

(ii) \(\mathrm{G}'\) is smooth.

(iii) For every integer \(n>0\), the sequence \[ 1 \longrightarrow {}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}\mathrm{G}' \xrightarrow{\ {}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}v\ } {}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}\mathrm{G} \xrightarrow{\ {}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}u\ } {}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}\mathrm{G}'' \longrightarrow 1 \] is exact.

(iv) The morphism \({}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}u : {}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}\mathrm{G} \to {}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}\mathrm{G}''\) is an epimorphism.

(v) The morphism \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(u) : \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}) \to \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}'')\) is surjective (II 4.11).

Then we have the implications (i) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (iv) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (v) and all the assertions are equivalent when G is smooth over \(k\).

Indeed, (i) is equivalent to (ii) by Exposé VI: recall indeed that (i) implies (ii) by SGA II 1.3; on the other hand (ii) means that \(u\) is smooth at the origin (SGA II 2.1; \(u\) is flat because it is epimorphic), hence everywhere.

Likewise, the equivalence of (iv) and (v) follows from the equivalence defined in 8.1 between the category of algebraic \(k\)-groups of height \(\leqslant 1\) and that of \(p\)-Lie algebras of finite dimension over \(k\).

The implication (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (iii) follows from the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ 1 \ar[r] & \mathrm{G}' \ar[r]^-{v} \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{G}'/k)} & \mathrm{G} \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{G}/k)} & \mathrm{G}'' \ar[r] \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{G}''/k)} & 1 \\ 1 \ar[r] & \mathrm{G}'^{(p^n)} \ar[r]_-{v^{(p^n)}} & \mathrm{G}^{(p^n)} \ar[r]_-{u^{(p^n)}} & \mathrm{G}''^{(p^n)} \ar[r] & 1 } \] whose two rows are exact: since \(\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{G}'/k)\) is an epimorphism by Corollary 8.3.1 below, \(u\) induces an epimorphism from \({}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}\mathrm{G}\) onto \({}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}\mathrm{G}''\) (generalize the snake lemma to sheaves of not necessarily commutative groups).

Finally, when G is smooth over \(k\), \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{G}/k)\) is an epimorphism. If, moreover, \({}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}u\) is an epimorphism, the same snake lemma applied to the diagram above for \(n=1\) shows that \(\mathrm{Fr}(\mathrm{G}'/k)\) is an epimorphism, hence that \(\mathrm{G}'\) is smooth over \(k\) (8.3.1 below).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.3

If G is an algebraic group over a field \(k\) of characteristic \(p>0\), there exists an integer \(n_0\) such that \(\mathrm{G}/{}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}\mathrm{G}\) is smooth over \(k\) for \(n \geqslant n_0\).

Since the construction of \(\mathrm{G}/{}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}\mathrm{G}\) commutes with extension of the base field (4.1.1 and VIA, 4.7), we may assume \(k\) perfect (SGA II 5.5). In this case, \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is an algebraic subgroup of G (VIA 0.2) and we have the commutative and exact diagram \[ \xymatrix{ 1 \ar[r] & \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}} \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}/k)} & \mathrm{G} \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{G}/k)} & \mathrm{H} \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{H}/k)} \\ 1 \ar[r] & \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}^{(p^n)} \ar[r] & \mathrm{G}^{(p^n)} \ar[r] & \mathrm{H}^{(p^n)}\ , } \] where we have set \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\backslash\mathrm{G}\). Now H is the spectrum of a finite local \(k\)-algebra with residue field \(k\) (VIB). Consequently, there exists an integer \(n_0\) such that \(\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{H}/k)\) factors through the unique section of \(\mathrm{Spec}\ k\) in \(\mathrm{H}^{(p^n)}\) for \(n\geqslant n_0\). It follows that, for \(n\geqslant n_0\), \(\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{G}/k)\) factors through \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}^{(p^n)}\); the homomorphism \(h : \mathrm{G}/{}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}\mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\), defined by this factorization, is a monomorphism (VIA 5.4) and induces a homeomorphism of the underlying topological spaces; it is therefore an isomorphism (VIB, \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}^{(p^n)}\) is reduced). Since \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}^{(p^n)}\) is smooth over \(k\) (VIA, 1.3.1), \(\mathrm{G}/{}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}\mathrm{G}\) is smooth over \(k\) for \(n\geqslant n_0\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.3.1

Let \(n\) be an integer \(\geqslant 1\). Then G is smooth over \(k\) if and only if \(\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{G}/k)\) is an epimorphism.
If G is smooth over \(k\), G is reduced and \(\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{G}/k)\) is surjective, hence is an epimorphism.

Conversely, since \(\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{G}/k)^{(p^n)}\) coincides with \(\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{G}^{(p^n)}/k)\) (see 4.1.3), \(\mathrm{Fr}^{nm}(\mathrm{G}/k)\) is an epimorphism for every \(m\) if \(\mathrm{Fr}^n(\mathrm{G}/k)\) is one. We then have \(\mathrm{G}/{}_{\mathrm{Fr}^{nm}}\mathrm{G} \simeq \mathrm{G}^{(p^{nm})}\). Since \(\mathrm{G}/{}_{\mathrm{Fr}^{nm}}\mathrm{G}\) is smooth over \(k\) for \(m\) large, \(\mathrm{G}^{(p^{nm})}\) and G are as well.
In the two statements that conclude this exposé, we return to the case of a field \(k\) of arbitrary characteristic.

When \(k\) is of characteristic 0 (resp. \(p>0\)), let \(n\) be an integer \(\geqslant 1\) (resp. an integer \(\geqslant 1\) and prime to \(p\)): in both cases, we simply say that \(n\) is prime to the characteristic of \(k\). Moreover, if G is a group scheme over \(k\), we denote by \(n_\mathrm{G} : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}\) the morphism of \(k\)-schemes that sends an element \(x\) of \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T})\) to \(x^n \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T})\), for T a \(k\)-scheme.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition

Let G be an algebraic group over a field \(k\) and \(n\) an integer prime to the characteristic of \(k\). Then \(n_\mathrm{G} : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}\) is a morphism that is étale at the origin.

Indeed, let A be the local ring of G at the origin and I the maximal ideal of A. By II 3.9, the map \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}\ n_\mathrm{G} : \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}\ \mathrm{G} \to \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}\ \mathrm{G}\) induced by \(n_\mathrm{G}\) is the homothety of ratio \(n\). It is therefore an isomorphism, as is the endomorphism induced by \(n_\mathrm{G}\) on \(\mathrm{I}/\mathrm{I}^2\). If \(k\) is of characteristic 0, G is smooth over \(k\) (VIB 1.6.1; see also VIIB §3); hence A is regular and \(n\) induces an automorphism of the graded object associated with A, hence also an automorphism of the completion \(\widehat{\mathrm{A}}\) of A.

If the characteristic is \(p>0\) and if G is of height \(\leqslant 1\), A is isomorphic to the quotient of the symmetric algebra of \(\omega_{\mathrm{G}/k} = \mathrm{I}/\mathrm{I}^2\) by the Ideal generated by the \(p\)-th powers of the elements of \(\omega_{\mathrm{G}/k}\) (7.4); one can then apply the "same" reasoning as in characteristic 0.

If G is of height \(\leqslant r\) and if we assume our assertion proved for groups of height \(\leqslant r-1\), let B, A and C be the affine algebras of \({}_{\mathrm{Fr}^r}\mathrm{G}\), G and \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{Fr}} = {}_{\mathrm{Fr}}\mathrm{G}\backslash\mathrm{G}\). Let us call \(n_\mathrm{B}\), \(n_\mathrm{A}\) and \(n_\mathrm{C}\) the morphisms of B, A and C induced by \(n_{{}_{\mathrm{Fr}^r}\mathrm{G}}\), \(n_\mathrm{G}\) and \(n_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{Fr}}}\). Since \(n_\mathrm{C}\) is an isomorphism by the induction hypothesis and A is flat over C (VIA 3.2), \(n_\mathrm{A}\) is a bijection if and only if \(n_\mathrm{A}\otimes_\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{C}/\mathfrak{r})\) is one (\(\mathfrak{r}\) denotes the radical of C); now \(n_\mathrm{A}\otimes_\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{C}/\mathfrak{r})\) is none other than \(n_\mathrm{B}\)!

Finally, when G is an arbitrary algebraic group over a field of characteristic \(p>0\), the above shows that \(n_\mathrm{G}\) induces automorphisms of the \(k\)-schemes \({}_{\mathrm{Fr}^r}\mathrm{G}\); these schemes are affine over \(k\) and have as algebras the quotients of the local algebra A by the ideal \(\mathrm{I}^{\{p^r\}}\) generated by the \(p^r\)-th powers of the elements of I. Since \(n_\mathrm{G}\) defines automorphisms of the algebras \(\mathrm{A}/\mathrm{I}^{\{p^r\}}\), one sees by passage to the projective limit that \(n\) induces an automorphism of \(\widehat{\mathrm{A}}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.5

Let G be a finite algebraic group of rank \(n\) over the field \(k\). Then \(n_\mathrm{G} : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}\) is the zero morphism of G (see 8.4).

Let F be a normal subgroup of G of rank \(m\) over \(k\). With the notation of VIA, 3.2, the square \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{F}\times\mathrm{G} \ar[r]^-{\lambda} \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{pr}_2} & \mathrm{G} \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{can.}} \\ \mathrm{G} \ar[r]_-{\mathrm{can.}} & \mathrm{F}\backslash\mathrm{G} } \] is cartesian. Since \(\mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{F}\backslash\mathrm{G}\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact (VIA 3.2), and \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) is locally free of rank \(m\), it follows from EGA IV 2.5.2 that \(\mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{F}\backslash\mathrm{G}\) is locally free of rank \(m\). We therefore have \(\mathrm{rg}_k(\mathrm{F}\backslash\mathrm{G}) \times \mathrm{rg}_k\mathrm{F} = \mathrm{rg}_k\mathrm{G}\).

On the other hand, we have an exact sequence of "abstract" groups \[ 1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{F}(\mathrm{T}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T}) \longrightarrow (\mathrm{F}\backslash\mathrm{G})(\mathrm{T}) \] for every \(k\)-scheme T; it is therefore clear that \(n_\mathrm{G}\) is zero if \(m_\mathrm{F}\) and \((nm^{-1})_{\mathrm{F}\backslash\mathrm{G}}\) are. If F is the connected component of the origin of G, \(\mathrm{F}\backslash\mathrm{G}\) is étale (VIB), so that we may assume G étale over \(k\) or connected.
If G is étale, one reduces, by extension of the base field, to the case where \(k\) is algebraically closed. In this case, G is a constant group (I 4.1), and the statement is classical.

If G is connected and nonzero, the characteristic \(p\) of \(k\) is necessarily \(>0\) (VIB; VIIB §3); the subgroups \({}_{\mathrm{Fr}^r}\mathrm{G}\) then form a composition series of G whose quotients are of height \(\leqslant 1\).

This reduces us to the case where G is of height \(\leqslant 1\): so let A be the affine algebra of G and L its Lie algebra; if \([\mathrm{L}:k]=r\), the rank of G over \(k\) is \(p^r\) (VIIA 5.3.3); we shall therefore study the morphism \(p_\mathrm{G} : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}\) defined by raising to the \(p\)-th power.

This morphism \(p_\mathrm{G}\) defines endomorphisms \(p_\mathrm{A}\) and \(p_\mathrm{U}\) of A and of the restricted enveloping algebra \(\mathrm{U} = \mathrm{U}_p(\mathrm{L})\) of L. The map \(p_\mathrm{U}\) decomposes as follows: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{U} \ar[r]^-{\Delta_\mathrm{U}^p} & \bigotimes\nolimits^p\mathrm{U} \ar[r]^-{m_\mathrm{U}^p} & \mathrm{U} } \] where \(\Delta_\mathrm{U}^p\) denotes the algebra homomorphism that sends \(x \in \mathrm{L}\subset \mathrm{U}\) to \(x\otimes 1\otimes\cdots\otimes 1 + 1\otimes x\otimes\cdots\otimes 1 + \cdots + 1\otimes 1\otimes\cdots\otimes x\), while \(m_\mathrm{U}^p\) is the linear map sending \(u_1\otimes u_2\otimes\cdots\otimes u_p\) to the product \(u_1u_2\cdots u_p\). If \(x_1,x_2,\ldots,x_t\) are \(t\) elements of \(\mathrm{L}\subset\mathrm{U}\), we thus have \[ p_\mathrm{U}(x_1x_2\cdots x_t) = m_\mathrm{U}^p\left(\ \prod_{j=1}^t\ \sum_{i=1}^p 1\otimes\cdots\otimes\overset{i}{x_j}\otimes\cdots\otimes 1\ \right) \]

It is clear that the expression \(\prod_j\sum_i 1\otimes\cdots\otimes x_j\otimes\cdots\otimes 1\) is a sum of \(p^t\) terms \(x_h\) indexed by the maps \(h\) from \(\{1,2,\ldots,t\}\) to \(\{1,2,\ldots,p\}\). Such a map defines a preorder on \(\{1,2,\ldots,t\}\) such that \(i\leqslant j\) if and only if \(h(i)\leqslant h(j)\); moreover, we have \(m_\mathrm{U}^p(x_h) = m_\mathrm{U}^p(x_\ell)\) if \(h\) and \(\ell\) define the same preorder, so that we may write \(m_\mathrm{U}^p(x_h) = x_\mathfrak{o}\), where \(\mathfrak{o}\) is the preorder defined by \(h\). We consequently have \[ p_\mathrm{U}(x_1x_2\cdots x_t) = \sum_\mathfrak{o} \binom{p}{s(\mathfrak{o})}\cdot x_\mathfrak{o}, \] where \(\mathfrak{o}\) runs through the preorder relations on \(\{1,\ldots,t\}\) such that the associated ordered set has at most \(p\) elements, and where \(s(\mathfrak{o})\) is the cardinality of the ordered set associated with \(\mathfrak{o}\).

When \(t < p\), all the terms \(\binom{p}{s(\mathfrak{o})}\) are zero, so that \(p_\mathrm{U}(x_1\cdots x_t) = 0\). In other words, \(p_\mathrm{U}\) vanishes on the vector subspace \(\mathrm{U}^+_{p-1}\) of U generated by the products \(x_1\cdots x_t\), \(t<p\), \(x_i\in\mathrm{L}\). Now, it follows easily from 7.3 that the canonical isomorphism from the dual \(\mathrm{U}^*\) onto A, described in 7.4, identifies the orthogonal of \(\mathrm{U}^+_{p-1}\) with \(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{A}^p\) (\(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{A}\) = augmentation ideal of A; see also VIIB 1.3.6 and 4.3). The isomorphism from \(\mathrm{U}^*\) onto A also allows one to identify \(p_\mathrm{A}\) with the transpose of \(p_\mathrm{U}\), so that the composite map \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{I}_\mathrm{A} \ar[r]^-{p_\mathrm{A}} & \mathrm{I}_\mathrm{A} \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{can.}} & \mathrm{I}_\mathrm{A}/\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{A}^p } \] is zero. Hence \(p_\mathrm{A}\) maps \(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{A}\) into \(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{A}^p\) and \((p_\mathrm{A})^r\) maps \(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{A}\) into \(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{A}^{p^r}\). Since \(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{A}^{r(p-1)+1}\) is zero by Theorem 7.4, the inequality \(p^r > r(p-1)\) shows that \(p_\mathrm{A}^r\) annihilates \(\mathrm{I}_\mathrm{A}\), q.e.d.
